proposals.title: DynamicSLAM: A Bayesian Forest Framework for Robust Localization in Non-Static Human Environments
proposals.motivation: Robust localization in dynamic environments, such as hospitals or public spaces, remains a critical challenge for autonomous systems. Pre-2026 SLAM methods often treat moving people and objects as outliers, degrading performance and safety. This work directly addresses the static world assumption, which is a fundamental limitation for real-world deployment of service robots and AR/VR applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling dynamic entities as part of a probabilistic, hierarchical scene representation, we can significantly improve localization accuracy and robustness compared to outlier-rejection methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid architecture combining a probabilistic, hierarchical scene graph with efficient decision forest-based classifiers. A base SLAM algorithm (e.g., ORB-SLAM3) will provide initial estimates. A multi-armed bandit algorithm (MABSplit) will efficiently manage a forest of Bayesian Decision Trees, trained to classify map features as static, dynamic, or semi-static based on spatio-temporal context. This forest will continuously update a tractable prior over scene dynamics, enabling the SLAM backend to marginalize out non-static elements while preserving their probabilistic structure for prediction.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a novel SLAM framework, DynamicSLAM, that maintains high accuracy in crowded, changing scenes. We will contribute a new public dataset of challenging indoor sequences with ground-truth dynamic object segmentation and a benchmark for dynamic SLAM. The work will demonstrate a principled integration of Bayesian non-parametric learning with geometric state estimation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using a bandit-optimized forest of Bayesian Decision Trees as a differentiable, adaptive front-end to model scene dynamics probabilistically, moving beyond simple filtering to an integrated, learnable perception-for-geometry pipeline.

proposals.title: Optimization-Guided Importance Sampling for Efficient Bayesian Inference in High-Dimensional Clinical Decision Models
proposals.motivation: Importance sampling is crucial for Bayesian inference in complex models, such as those used in clinical decision support, but its efficiency degrades in high dimensions if the proposal distribution poorly matches the target posterior. Pre-2026, generating good proposals often relies on heuristics or requires extensive tuning, limiting the practical application of probabilistic models in time-sensitive, data-driven healthcare settings where accuracy and computational performance are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that performing a rapid, local optimization scan (e.g., using stochastic gradient methods) to identify modes and curvature of the target log-posterior can systematically inform the parameters of a tractable, multi-modal proposal distribution (e.g., a mixture of Gaussians or Bayesian decision trees), leading to a dramatic reduction in the variance of importance sampling estimates.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithm that first performs a lightweight, multi-start optimization scan using techniques like Adam or limited-memory BFGS on a subset of the clinical dataset. The discovered modes and Hessian information will parameterize a primary proposal distribution. Inspired by MABSplit, a multi-armed bandit will dynamically allocate computational resources between refining this proposal via further local scans and performing the importance sampling itself. The proposal family will include flexible options like Bayesian decision tree ensembles, leveraging Thrun's prior work on tractable priors for trees.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated bridge between optimization and Monte Carlo methods, yielding a robust tool for faster and more accurate Bayesian inference in high-dimensional problems. It will directly enhance the performance of probabilistic clinical decision models by making posterior estimation more computationally feasible, enabling real-time uncertainty quantification.
proposals.novelty: While optimization is often used for mode-finding and variational inference, its systematic use to construct *direct* proposal distributions for importance sampling is novel. The integration of bandit-based resource allocation to balance proposal refinement and sampling creates an adaptive, efficient meta-algorithm distinct from existing MCMC or vanilla importance sampling methods.

proposals.title: Kernel Density Belief Trees: A Probabilistic Framework for Continuous Action Selection in Clinical Decision Support
proposals.motivation: Decision trees and forests are widely used in clinical decision support due to their interpretability and strong performance on tabular datasets. However, their discrete, axis-aligned splits produce piecewise-constant predictions, which can be suboptimal for representing nuanced, continuous patient states and uncertainties. A method to smooth these discrete outputs into a continuous belief distribution would better reflect clinical reality and enable more refined decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying kernel density estimation (KDE) to the sample distributions in the leaf nodes of a Bayesian decision tree will yield a more accurate and calibrated continuous representation of predictive uncertainty than standard probabilistic outputs, leading to improved performance in sequential clinical decision tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the Bayesian Decision Tree framework with tractable priors. For a given test point, we will aggregate the weighted samples from all leaves (using the tree's predictive posterior) rather than using a single leaf. A multivariate KDE will be applied to this aggregated sample set to form a smooth, continuous belief density over the output space (e.g., a treatment effect). For forest-scale efficiency, we will integrate MABSplit principles for tree induction and employ fast maximum inner product search techniques to accelerate kernel evaluations in high-dimensional feature spaces relevant to clinical data.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, hybrid tree-KDE model that maintains interpretability while providing smooth density estimates. It will offer a principled, non-parametric method for uncertainty quantification in decision forests, directly applicable to dynamic treatment regimes and personalized medicine. We will release an optimized implementation and benchmark it on real-world clinical datasets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of KDE directly into the Bayesian tree inference loop to create a continuous belief from the *entire generative model's* samples, moving beyond simple leaf histograms. This combines the structured learning of decision trees with the flexible representation of non-parametric density estimation in a computationally tractable way, which is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: MABForest-MHT: Multi-Armed Bandit Forests for Scalable Multi-Hypothesis Tracking in Cluttered Environments
proposals.motivation: Robustly tracking multiple, interacting dynamic objects is a core challenge for autonomous systems in navigation and healthcare monitoring. Traditional Multi-Hypothesis Tracking (MHT) suffers from combinatorial explosion in data association hypotheses, limiting real-time performance. This problem is acute in clinical or domestic settings (e.g., monitoring patient mobility) where occlusions and unpredictable motion are common, demanding algorithms that are both accurate and computationally tractable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a forest of decision trees, trained online with a multi-armed bandit strategy for efficient feature selection, can learn to prune the MHT hypothesis tree more effectively than hand-crafted rules or generic classifiers, dramatically improving tracking accuracy and speed in high-clutter scenarios.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MCL paradigm by treating data association as a sequential classification problem for each sensor observation. A random forest, where each tree is a 'hypothesis evaluator,' will be trained to predict the correct object association using kinematic and appearance features. Inspired by MABSplit, we will employ contextual bandits at each node to dynamically select the most informative features for splitting during online forest updates, reducing the need for exhaustive search. The forest's probabilistic outputs will directly weight and prune the MHT hypothesis tree, with efficient maximum inner product search (MIPS) techniques used to accelerate nearest-neighbor queries for appearance-based features.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, learning-based MHT framework that tightly integrates fast forest training with probabilistic tracking. We will release a benchmark dataset for multi-object tracking in cluttered indoor environments. The algorithm will provide a significant speed-accuracy Pareto frontier improvement over classical MHT and recent learning-aided trackers, validated on clinical mobility and warehouse robot datasets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct synthesis of online bandit-optimized decision forests with the MHT framework for adaptive hypothesis pruning, moving beyond using learned models only for feature scoring or single-hypothesis prediction.

proposals.title: Probabilistic Hierarchical Planning for Large Autonomous Vessels via Bayesian Decision Forests and Multi-Armed Bandits
proposals.motivation: The autonomous shipping industry is poised for significant growth, offering efficiency and safety benefits. However, large vessels present a unique challenge: their enormous inertia, slow dynamics, and long sensor ranges create a planning problem where decisions must be made under extreme uncertainty over very long horizons. Pre-2026 planning methods for cars or drones do not scale to these spatiotemporal dimensions and risk profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical planning framework, using Bayesian decision trees to model high-level route options and multi-armed bandits for real-time tactical adjustments, will yield safer and more fuel-efficient navigation policies for large autonomous ships than conventional optimization or monolithic learning approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tier probabilistic planner. At the high level, we adapt Bayesian Decision Trees, with priors defined via Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars over nautical routes, to evaluate coarse, long-horizon options. At the low level, we employ MABSplit-inspired multi-armed bandits to manage frequent, fine-grained decisions (e.g., minor course adjustments) within each high-level option, using a learned model of vessel dynamics and environmental uncertainty. For efficient real-time inference in this high-dimensional state-action space, we will integrate fast similarity search techniques akin to Maximum Inner Product Search.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable planning architecture specifically designed for the unique constraints of large autonomous vessels. It will contribute a new benchmark dataset of simulated maritime scenarios and demonstrate quantifiable improvements in safety (reduced collision risk) and operational efficiency (fuel savings) over baseline methods.
proposals.novelty: The integration of Bayesian tree-structured priors for long-horizon maritime route reasoning with bandit-based tactical controllers is unexplored. This moves beyond applying single-scale RL or deterministic path planning to a domain requiring explicit multi-fidelity uncertainty management.

proposals.title: Closing the Implementation Gap in Clinical ML: An Immersive Training Framework for Decision Tree Ensembles
proposals.motivation: A persistent gap exists between the accuracy of machine learning algorithms on benchmark datasets and their real-world clinical performance, particularly for interpretable models like decision trees and forests. This 'implementation gap' limits trust and deployment in high-stakes medical settings. While creating more benchmarks is a common response, it does not directly address the core issue of translating model outputs into actionable, reliable clinical workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an immersive training paradigm, where tree-based models are co-optimized with a simulated clinical decision environment, can significantly reduce the implementation gap by improving robustness, calibration, and actionability beyond what is captured by standard accuracy metrics on static datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation environment modeling clinical workflows and uncertainty. Using a multi-armed bandit framework (inspired by MABSplit) for efficient tree induction, we will train Bayesian decision tree ensembles with priors favoring clinically interpretable structures. The model's training objective will be extended via reinforcement learning to maximize long-term simulated patient outcomes within the immersive environment, not just immediate prediction accuracy. This integrates probabilistic reasoning (from Thrun's Bayesian tree work) with bandit-based optimization for a novel training loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new methodology for training clinically-actionable tree models and a quantitative framework for measuring the implementation gap. It will contribute a publicly available clinical simulation toolkit for immersive ML training, moving beyond static benchmarks. The resulting models are expected to demonstrate superior real-world readiness in pilot studies on skin cancer diagnosis datasets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reframing the implementation gap as a training problem solvable through immersive simulation, rather than a benchmarking problem. It uniquely combines Bayesian tree priors, bandit-based forest training, and reinforcement learning in a clinical context to optimize for deployment utility, not just dataset accuracy.

proposals.title: Curriculum Learning for Clinical Decision Forests: A Benchmark Suite of Structured Complexity
proposals.motivation: Clinical decision support systems, particularly those based on tree ensembles, are often evaluated on static datasets, failing to capture the progressive learning curve of medical practitioners. This mismatch limits our understanding of how algorithmic performance scales with problem difficulty, a critical factor for robust deployment in healthcare. A structured benchmark that mirrors the pedagogical progression from simple to complex clinical scenarios is needed to train and evaluate more adaptive, human-aligned models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that structuring clinical benchmark tasks into a curriculum of increasing diagnostic and prognostic complexity will enable the training of more robust and sample-efficient decision forest models, and that this curriculum-based evaluation will reveal failure modes invisible to standard, flat benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will design a modular benchmark suite where tasks (e.g., skin lesion classification, patient risk stratification) are parameterized by complexity dimensions such as feature ambiguity, label noise, and dataset shift. Drawing on Thrun's work on efficient forest training (MABSplit) and Bayesian Decision Trees, we will develop a curriculum learning scheduler that uses model confidence or performance on simpler tasks to gate progression to harder ones. The training efficiency of this curriculum-guided approach will be compared against standard training on the aggregate dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, publicly available benchmark for clinical machine learning that emphasizes progressive difficulty. It will provide a framework for evaluating the sample efficiency and robustness of decision forest algorithms under structured complexity, moving beyond singular accuracy metrics. The findings could inform the development of training protocols for more reliable clinical AI.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning has been explored in deep learning, its systematic application to benchmark design and training for interpretable models like decision forests in the clinical domain is novel. The integration of complexity-parameterized tasks with bandit-based forest training procedures (MABSplit) for curriculum scheduling is a unique methodological fusion.

proposals.title: EM-TreeSLAM: Expectation-Maximization for Globally Consistent, Offline Map and Trajectory Refinement
proposals.motivation: Offline Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) is critical for creating high-precision maps for autonomous vehicles and robotics from pre-recorded sensor logs. However, existing batch optimization methods, like pose-graph SLAM, can struggle with global consistency in large-scale, feature-poor environments and often treat data association as a separate, brittle step. A unified probabilistic framework for joint data association and state estimation could yield more accurate and globally consistent maps.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating offline SLAM as a latent variable problem solvable via Expectation-Maximization (EM) will enable more robust global optimization by probabilistically reasoning over data associations (E-step) and jointly refining the entire robot trajectory and map (M-step) in a single coherent framework.
proposals.method: We will develop EM-TreeSLAM, an algorithm where the E-step computes soft assignments between observations and map landmarks using a probabilistic model, informed by the current map estimate. The M-step will perform maximum a posteriori estimation of the full trajectory and landmark positions, formulated as a nonlinear least-squares problem solvable via efficient sparse solvers like g2o. To manage computational complexity in the data association search, we will integrate techniques from high-dimensional similarity search, inspired by Thrun's work on Maximum Inner Product Search, to accelerate the E-step. The map representation will leverage a hierarchical, tree-based structure to enable efficient multi-scale matching and updates.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, probabilistically grounded EM algorithm for offline SLAM that jointly optimizes data association and state estimation. It will provide a robust baseline for creating globally consistent maps from challenging datasets, with measurable improvements in accuracy over decoupled approaches. The released open-source implementation will serve as a valuable tool for the robotics and computer vision communities.
proposals.novelty: While EM has a long history in specific SLAM contexts (e.g., EM for occupancy grids), its application as a unified framework for feature-based, large-scale offline SLAM with integrated high-dimensional data association is novel. The key innovation is the tight coupling of probabilistic data association (E-step) with full batch trajectory and map optimization (M-step) within a single tree-managed spatial model.

proposals.title: BANDIT-SLAM: Multi-Armed Bandit Decision Trees for Active Exploration Under Pose Graph Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Active SLAM is critical for long-term autonomy in unknown environments, where a robot must intelligently trade off exploration and map refinement. Current frontier-based or information-gain methods often rely on expensive, full posterior updates over the entire pose graph, which scales poorly for large, complex environments and limits real-time decision-making. A more efficient, data-driven approach to action selection is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling the robot's local decision context as a set of bandit arms, where each arm represents a potential action's expected reduction in pose graph uncertainty, a lightweight, learned decision tree policy can outperform classical utility calculations in both speed and final map accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop BANDIT-SLAM, which integrates a probabilistic pose graph SLAM backend (e.g., GTSAM) with a frontend decision agent. The agent will use a novel MABSplit-Tree algorithm, extending MABSplit for forest training. At each step, features describing local map entropy, loop closure potential, and trajectory consistency form the context for a multi-armed bandit. A decision tree ensemble, trained offline on diverse simulation datasets, will rapidly select the action (bandit arm) that maximizes the expected information gain, approximated via efficient posterior uncertainty metrics from the SLAM system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, scalable framework for Active SLAM that replaces costly online utility optimization with fast, learned decision tree inference, and 2) a new MABSplit-Tree algorithm tailored for sequential decision-making under uncertainty, evaluated on established robotic datasets. We will provide open-source code and benchmark performance against state-of-the-art methods like Monte Carlo Tree Search and Cauchy-Schwarz quadratic mutual information.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of bandit-based decision tree learning—inspired by Thrun's recent work on efficient forests—with probabilistic SLAM backends for active exploration, creating a hybrid model-based and data-driven planner that is both theoretically grounded and computationally efficient.

proposals.title: Robust and Adaptive State Estimation via Variational t-Distributions for Robotic Learning
proposals.motivation: State estimation in robotics, from Kalman filtering to factor-graph SLAM, traditionally relies on Gaussian noise assumptions, which are fragile to real-world outliers in sensor data and motion dynamics. This fragility limits the reliability and safety of autonomous systems operating in unstructured environments. Drawing inspiration from robust econometrics, we hypothesize that heavy-tailed distributions can provide a principled, model-based defense against these outliers.
proposals.hypothesis: Modeling sensor and process noise with a Student's t-distribution, and jointly inferring its degrees-of-freedom parameter via variational inference, will yield a state estimator with superior robustness and comparable computational efficiency to standard Gaussian-based methods.
proposals.method: We will formulate a robust state estimation framework where observation and transition likelihoods are scaled Student's t-distributions. To enable tractable online inference, we will develop a structured mean-field variational algorithm that approximates the joint posterior over the latent state trajectory and the t-distribution parameters (degrees of freedom, scale). This approach will be integrated into a factor-graph optimization backend (e.g., GTSAM) for SLAM and a recursive filter formulation for online tracking, leveraging automatic differentiation for gradient-based optimization of the variational objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, robust state estimation algorithm that automatically adapts to outlier severity, reducing the need for manual sensor filtering or heuristic outlier rejection. It will provide the robotics community with a principled Bayesian tool, backed by strong empirical performance on challenging real-world datasets, to improve the reliability of perception and control.
proposals.novelty: While t-distributions have been used in robust filtering, the integration of adaptive, variational inference for the degrees-of-freedom parameter within modern factor-graph-based state estimation frameworks is novel. This moves beyond fixed-parameter robust models to a fully adaptive, learning-based filter.

proposals.title: MABSplit for Hypothesis Forests: Efficient Hierarchical World Modeling for Robust Data Association
proposals.motivation: Robust data association under severe perceptual aliasing is a critical unsolved problem for long-term autonomy in robotics and augmented reality. Current methods, like single-hypothesis probabilistic filters, often fail catastrophically in repetitive or symmetric environments (e.g., warehouses, office corridors). A principled, computationally tractable method to maintain and manage multiple global association hypotheses is needed to prevent these failures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, tree-based approach to generating and pruning global data association hypotheses, combined with efficient multi-armed bandit resource allocation across parallel world models, will yield a system that is both robust to aliasing and computationally sustainable for real-time operation.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-stage pipeline. First, we will use fast, non-parametric tests (e.g., topological consistency, viewpoint geometry from cheap depth sensors) to generate candidate global associations, structured as a hypothesis tree. Second, we will adapt the MABSplit algorithm to efficiently allocate computational resources (e.g., particle filter updates) across the most promising branches of this tree, treating each branch as an arm. Third, we will instantiate separate Bayesian decision trees or probabilistic filters for each surviving hypothesis. Finally, an entropy-based criterion will guide hypothesis merging or trigger active information gathering.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable framework for robust simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) and object-level tracking in ambiguous environments. It will provide a concrete algorithm merging ideas from multi-armed bandits for resource-aware search with hierarchical probabilistic world models, directly addressing a key brittleness in modern robotic perception.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of efficient bandit-based resource allocation (MABSplit) into the hypothesis management layer of a multi-hypothesis data association system, creating a 'hypothesis forest' that dynamically focuses computation on the most plausible world models. This moves beyond static branching factors or fixed-horizon hypothesis trees.

proposals.title: Open-World Landmark Discovery via Zero-Shot Prototype Clustering and Decision-Theoretic Novelty Detection
proposals.motivation: Current robotic and vision systems for semantic mapping rely on closed-world assumptions, failing to recognize or categorize landmarks not in their pre-trained ontology. This limits long-term autonomy in dynamic, unstructured environments. Drawing an analogy to metagenomic discovery of unknown microbial taxa, we address the need for systems that can continuously expand their semantic understanding without exhaustive retraining.
proposals.hypothesis: By learning a joint visual-textual embedding space from a broad but incomplete set of known landmarks, a system can cluster novel visual structures and, through statistical decision theory, reliably identify and propose semantic labels for genuinely new landmark categories.
proposals.method: We will train a vision-language foundation model (e.g., CLIP variant) on datasets like Google Landmarks and OpenImages to establish a prototype space. For inference, we extract visual features from a scene and perform fast clustering (using MABSplit for efficient forest-based partitioning). Novelty detection will be framed as a Bayesian decision problem, comparing cluster statistics to known prototypes to decide if a cluster represents a new category. For novel clusters, we will generate descriptive labels via prompting the text encoder.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a formal framework and algorithm for open-world landmark semantics, enabling continuous learning in mapping. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating open-world discovery in robotics and computer vision, moving beyond fixed-class accuracy metrics.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the adaptation of metagenomic analysis pipelines—specifically marker-based clustering and novelty detection—to visual landmark discovery, combined with a decision-theoretic approach to manage the risk of false discoveries, which is under-explored in open-world vision.

proposals.title: Bayesian Forest SLAM: A Multi-Layer World Model with Probabilistic Dynamic Entity Forecasting
proposals.motivation: Current SLAM systems struggle in dynamic environments, often treating moving objects as noise that corrupts the static map. For autonomous systems operating in crowded, real-world settings like hospitals or public streets, robustly distinguishing static, semi-static, and dynamic elements is critical for safe navigation and long-term operation. This problem remains a key bottleneck for real-world deployment of mobile robots and AR/VR systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a SLAM framework using a hierarchical, probabilistic world model—explicitly separating static, semi-static, and dynamic layers with learned motion priors—will achieve superior robustness and ego-motion accuracy in complex dynamic scenes compared to monolithic or purely geometric approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-layer mapping back-end using an ensemble of Bayesian decision trees (inspired by Thrun's prior work) to probabilistically classify map elements into static, semi-static, or dynamic categories based on spatio-temporal feature consistency. Dynamic entities will be tracked using a multi-hypothesis filter (MABSplit for efficient data association) and their motion will be forecasted using compact, learned probabilistic context-free grammars over trajectories. The static map will be updated using only features classified as persistent, preventing corruption.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, real-time SLAM architecture that provides explicit, forecastable representations of dynamic entities. It will deliver a new public dataset with dense annotations for dynamic SLAM evaluation and demonstrate significant improvements in localization accuracy and map consistency on benchmarks like KITTI and in novel crowded indoor environments.
proposals.novelty: The integration of Bayesian decision tree ensembles for persistent feature classification within SLAM, combined with the use of probabilistic grammars for forecasting dynamic object motion, represents a novel synthesis of Thrun's core themes (trees, learning, algorithms) with the dynamic SLAM problem.

proposals.title: Bayesian Online Adaptation of Learned Sensor-Motion Models for Robust Long-Term Robot Autonomy
proposals.motivation: For robots deployed over months or years, such as in homes or hospitals, sensor readings and actuator responses inevitably drift due to wear, environmental changes, or component degradation. Pre-deployment models become inaccurate, leading to catastrophic failures. Current methods for online adaptation often lack principled uncertainty quantification and efficient data selection, limiting safe, long-term operation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical Bayesian framework, combining learned decision trees for interpretable sensor/motion models with online bandit algorithms for efficient data acquisition, will enable robust, sample-efficient, and uncertainty-aware adaptation for long-term autonomy.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tiered online learning system. First, we will use probabilistic context-free grammars to define priors over interpretable Bayesian decision tree structures for sensor and motion models, as in Thrun's prior work. Second, we will integrate a Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) algorithm, inspired by MABSplit, to actively select which parts of the model to update based on predictive uncertainty and potential performance gain, prioritizing informative data from the ongoing deployment. This enables efficient, continual refinement of the models.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified algorithm for continual model adaptation in long-term autonomous systems. It will provide a clinical, rigorous framework for evaluating adaptation performance on real-robot datasets, measuring accuracy retention and sample efficiency. The result will be a principled approach to maintaining robot performance in the face of real-world change.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of structured Bayesian priors for interpretable models with bandit-based active learning for online adaptation, creating a closed-loop system for long-term autonomy that balances model complexity, uncertainty, and data efficiency in a non-stationary environment.

proposals.title: Adversarial Navigation via Game-Theoretic Decision Forests with Online Bandit Learning
proposals.motivation: Autonomous navigation in shared environments (e.g., warehouses, public sidewalks) increasingly involves interaction with other agents, some of which may behave adversarially or competitively. Pre-2026 planning methods often assume cooperative or predictable agents, leaving systems vulnerable to strategic interference. A robust, real-time decision-making framework is needed for safe and efficient navigation in these mixed-motive settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling adversarial navigation as a repeated, imperfect-information game and solving it with an ensemble of game-theoretic decision trees, optimized online via multi-armed bandits, will yield significantly more robust and computationally efficient policies than standard reinforcement learning or deterministic planning approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-level hierarchical model. First, a forest of Bayesian decision trees, guided by probabilistic context-free grammar priors (from Thrun's prior work), will represent diverse navigation strategies and opponent models. Second, a multi-armed bandit algorithm (MABSplit) will be adapted for online selection and adaptation of the most promising tree-based policy in response to an adversary's observed actions, framed as a sequential game. Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) will accelerate the retrieval of similar game states from a historical dataset of interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel fusion of ensemble tree methods with game-theoretic reasoning for real-world adversarial planning, 2) a new online bandit-based algorithm for dynamic strategy selection in adversarial environments, and 3) a public benchmark dataset and clinical simulation framework for evaluating the safety and performance of such interactive navigation systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating Bayesian decision forests—typically used for static classification—into a dynamic, game-theoretic planning loop and using bandit algorithms not just for tree training but for real-time meta-policy selection against an adaptive adversary, a departure from monolithic neural network policies.

proposals.title: Online Variational Meta-Selection of Heavy-Tailed Noise Models for Robust State Estimation
proposals.motivation: Robust state estimation in robotics and autonomous systems is critically undermined by non-Gaussian sensor and motion noise, such as outliers and multi-modal disturbances. While heavy-tailed distributions (e.g., Student's t, Laplace) and their mixtures offer robustness, selecting and adapting the correct noise model online remains a challenging, unsolved model selection problem. Current methods often rely on fixed, hand-tuned assumptions, limiting performance and adaptability in real-world, non-stationary environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that placing a Bayesian nonparametric prior over a flexible family of heavy-tailed distributions and performing joint online inference over both the system state and the noise model structure will yield more robust and adaptive state estimation than methods with a fixed noise assumption.
proposals.method: We will develop an online variational inference framework where the state and noise model are co-estimated. A Dirichlet Process prior will be placed over a dictionary of noise components (e.g., Gaussian, Student's t, Laplace). The inference will use a structured mean-field approximation, with the state posterior approximated by a Gaussian and the noise model posterior by a truncated stick-breaking process. Key algorithmic innovations will include integrating bandit-style exploration (inspired by MABSplit) for efficient component selection and leveraging fast inner-product search for evaluating likelihoods under many candidate models in high-dimensional latent spaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a computationally tractable, online algorithm for joint state estimation and noise model selection with formal uncertainty quantification. It will provide a principled, automated alternative to manual noise model tuning, advancing robust perception for autonomous systems operating in adversarial or anomalous conditions. The resulting software framework will be validated on clinical motion capture and autonomous driving datasets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the online, joint Bayesian nonparametric treatment of noise model selection as an integral part of state estimation, moving beyond fixed models or simple switching mechanisms. This combines flexible nonparametric priors with efficient online variational inference and bandit-inspired exploration in a way not previously explored for this problem.

proposals.title: PhyloLandmark: Joint Probabilistic Inference of Latent Structure and Data Association via Phylogenetic Alignment
proposals.motivation: In robotics (e.g., SLAM) and medical imaging (e.g., cell tracking in microscopy), a core challenge is associating observed features or landmarks across multiple views when their identities are fundamentally unknown. Current methods, like the Hungarian algorithm or probabilistic data association filters, often make hard assignments or rely on heuristics, failing to properly marginalize over the combinatorial space of possible correspondences, which leads to brittle estimates and unquantified uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing the multi-view landmark association problem as a statistical phylogenetics task—where observations are analogous to species and the latent landmark set is analogous to an ancestral sequence—we can develop a joint probabilistic model that, by marginalizing over all possible alignments (homologies), will yield more robust estimates of the underlying structure and a principled measure of correspondence uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will construct a generative model where each observed landmark set is a 'leaf' generated via a stochastic process (e.g., a substitution model for positions plus an insertion/deletion process) from a latent 'ancestral' landmark configuration. Inference will use a combination of techniques: a probabilistic context-free grammar (PCFG), inspired by Thrun's prior work on Bayesian trees, to model the structural alignment space, and Monte Carlo methods (e.g., MCMC) to sample over tree topologies and alignments. The model will be trained and evaluated on clinical datasets where ground truth is sparse, using accuracy and calibration of uncertainty as key performance metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified framework for simultaneous structure estimation and data association with inherent uncertainty quantification. It will deliver a new algorithmic tool for domains like unsupervised landmark discovery in developmental biology or robust loop closure in SLAM, moving beyond hard-assignment bottlenecks.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic alignment is a mature field, its direct application to the geometric data association problem in robotics and computer vision is novel. The key innovation is treating landmark coordinates as evolvable 'traits' and using the full machinery of statistical phylogenetics to marginalize over the alignment, a perspective not found in standard computer vision or tracking literature.

proposals.title: Robust Topological Navigation via Bayesian Local Metric Verification
proposals.motivation: Topological maps offer efficient, scalable navigation for robots and autonomous agents but are fundamentally brittle to perceptual aliasing and environmental change, where distinct locations appear similar. This vulnerability limits their deployment in real-world, long-term applications. A hybrid approach that retains topological efficiency while embedding local metric verification can directly address this core weakness.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting topological graph nodes with lightweight, probabilistic metric models will enable robust disambiguation during navigation, significantly reducing catastrophic localization failures due to aliasing or dynamics without sacrificing the scalability of topological planning.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical world model where a coarse topological graph is constructed using learned place descriptors. Each node will be augmented with a local probabilistic metric model, implemented as a compact Gaussian Mixture Model over 3D point features or a learned embedding from a lightweight convolutional network. During navigation, long-horizon planning uses the graph. Upon reaching a hypothesized node, we employ a fast probabilistic inference step, inspired by Bayesian decision tree principles, to compute the likelihood of the current observation under the node's local model versus neighboring models, triggering graph updates or re-localization as needed. Techniques like MABSplit will be adapted to optimize the verification process.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, robust navigation framework that seamlessly combines topological and metric representations. It will provide a principled, probabilistic solution to the aliasing problem, enabling long-term autonomy in changing environments. The resulting algorithm will be evaluated on challenging robotic and simulated navigation datasets to demonstrate improved accuracy and reliability.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight integration of a probabilistic verification mechanism directly into the topological navigation loop, moving beyond simple descriptor matching to a Bayesian decision-theoretic framework for local disambiguation, which is underexplored in pre-2026 topological mapping literature.

proposals.title: Consensus-Guided Open-World Landmark Learning with Bayesian Hypothesis Testing for Robust Long-Term Autonomy
proposals.motivation: For autonomous systems operating in open-world environments, the ability to safely discover and integrate new semantic categories is critical for long-term deployment. Current semantic mapping and landmark recognition systems are brittle, relying on closed-world assumptions; they either fail to adapt to novelty or risk catastrophic semantic contamination from perceptual outliers or adversarial inputs. This limits the reliability of robots and autonomous vehicles in dynamic, unstructured settings.
proposals.hypothesis: That a landmark acquisition system governed by criteria of persistent utility and multi-agent consensus, formalized within a Bayesian probabilistic world model, can enable safe, evidence-based discovery of novel landmark categories while maintaining robustness against semantic contamination.
proposals.method: We will develop a system where potential new landmarks are treated as hypotheses within a probabilistic context-free grammar prior, akin to Bayesian Decision Trees, allowing for structured class generation. A multi-armed bandit framework (MABSplit) will efficiently allocate resources to evaluate candidate landmarks based on persistence and functional utility for navigation tasks. Consensus across multiple agent instances or temporal slices will be required for class promotion, validated against a held-out clinical dataset for real-world performance and accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable framework for open-world semantic mapping that explicitly balances adaptability with safety. It will deliver a new algorithm for landmark acquisition with proven robustness metrics, advancing the field of long-term autonomy. The resulting system will be evaluated on autonomous driving and mobile robot datasets to demonstrate improved task performance in novel environments.
proposals.novelty: The integration of consensus mechanisms and persistent utility into a Bayesian hypothesis-testing framework for landmark discovery is novel, moving beyond single-agent anomaly detection. It uniquely combines probabilistic grammar priors with bandit-based resource allocation specifically for the problem of semantic class formation.

proposals.title: MABSplit-Forests for Active Open-World Semantic Mapping: An Information-Theoretic Policy for Ambiguity Resolution and Novelty Detection
proposals.motivation: Robots operating in unstructured, real-world environments must build semantic maps, but pre-defined object categories are insufficient. Pre-2026 methods for open-world learning or active perception typically treat these as separate problems, leading to inefficient data collection and an inability to strategically manage the exploration-exploitation trade-off between confirming known landmarks and discovering novel ones. This limits long-term autonomy in domains like field robotics or assistive care.
proposals.hypothesis: An active perception policy, grounded in information theory and implemented with an ensemble of online decision trees, can optimally direct a robot's sensing actions to simultaneously reduce uncertainty about known landmark categories and maximize information gain for discovering and initially characterizing novel semantic classes.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic forest (e.g., using Bayesian Decision Tree priors) as the agent's internal world model, continuously updated from streaming sensor data. The policy will treat viewpoint selection as a multi-armed bandit problem, building upon the MABSplit algorithm for efficient tree training. The dual objective will be formalized via a composite reward function: one term from the entropy reduction within the forest's known-class predictions, and another from the predictive uncertainty or 'out-of-distribution' score (inspired by maximum inner product search techniques for anomaly detection) to target novel observations. The agent will actively gather data to resolve splits in its decision trees.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified algorithmic framework for active open-world semantic mapping, demonstrated on a robotic navigation dataset. It will advance online, continual learning for robotics by providing a principled, information-theoretic control strategy. The integration of bandit-based tree learning with active perception for novelty discovery is a novel methodological contribution.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight coupling of an online, interpretable forest model with an active control policy explicitly designed for a dual information objective (known-class refinement and novel-class discovery), moving beyond passive open-world recognition or active perception with a fixed label set.

proposals.title: Decentralized, Real-Time Dynamic Scene Graphs for Urban Swarms via Bandit-Optimized Forest Fusion
proposals.motivation: Current multi-agent SLAM systems either build static maps or operate in small, coordinated fleets, failing to provide a scalable, city-scale, and live model of dynamic urban environments. This limits the potential for applications like cooperative autonomous driving and real-time digital twins, which require persistent, multi-perspective tracking of both infrastructure and non-stationary objects across a vast metropolitan area.
proposals.hypothesis: A decentralized framework that fuses real-time agent observations via a probabilistic, hierarchical scene graph structure can maintain a globally consistent, city-scale dynamic map, and that multi-armed bandit algorithms can optimally manage computational resources for data association and fusion across the swarm.
proposals.method: We propose a hierarchical, probabilistic scene graph where nodes represent entities (static/dynamic) and edges represent spatial/temporal relations. Each agent runs a local instance, building sub-graphs via monocular or LiDAR SLAM. For decentralized fusion, we adapt the MABSplit algorithm to function as a bandit-driven tree for efficient, real-time matching and merging of graph sub-structures across agents, minimizing communication. Bayesian Decision Trees with tractable priors will model object permanence and state prediction within the graph. Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) acceleration will enable fast similarity queries for loop closure and object re-identification across the swarm.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) a novel decentralized architecture for scalable, dynamic scene graph construction, 2) a bandit-optimized forest for efficient multi-agent data fusion, and 3) an open-source dataset and benchmark for city-scale dynamic multi-agent SLAM, advancing the state of cooperative perception.
proposals.novelty: The integration of bandit-driven decision forests for real-time, decentralized graph fusion represents a significant departure from centralized servers or consensus-based approaches, enabling scalable, live updates to a unified dynamic world model.

proposals.title: Lifelong, Self-Tuning World Models via Bayesian Nonparametric Learning and Decision Forests
proposals.motivation: Long-term robot autonomy in open-world environments requires systems that can learn continuously and adapt their internal structure without human intervention. Current approaches, which rely on fixed parametric models or periodic retraining, fail to scale over years as they cannot discover new persistent entities or forget obsolete information, leading to catastrophic performance degradation. A unified framework for lifelong, self-structuring world modeling is a critical unsolved challenge for deploying robust robotic assistants in homes, hospitals, or cities.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified probabilistic framework that jointly learns sensor/motion models via online Bayesian inference and structures its internal world representation via Bayesian nonparametric processes will enable robust, scalable lifelong autonomy, outperforming fixed-model and periodic-retraining baselines in long-term deployment.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model where a Dirichlet Process (DP) mixture model clusters sensory data into latent landmarks and objects, with a Beta Process (BP) governing their persistence and appearance over time. This nonparametric representation will be coupled with an online-learned sensor model and a motion model, both updated via stochastic variational inference. For efficient spatial reasoning and decision-making within this growing model, we will integrate fast, probabilistic decision forests (inspired by MABSplit and Bayesian Decision Trees) for tasks like data association and maximum inner product search for landmark matching.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, unified algorithmic framework for lifelong world modeling that jointly tunes parameters and evolves structure, 2) an open-source system validated on long-term robot datasets, and 3) new performance benchmarks for lifelong autonomy, measuring accuracy and coherence over extended periods.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, online coupling of nonparametric structure learning with model parameter learning within a single Bayesian framework, enabling a robot to autonomously decide when to create a new concept, merge two concepts, or forget one—a capability beyond mere parameter adaptation or offline restructuring.

proposals.title: Task-Optimal Information Gathering via Decision-Theoretic Mutual Information for Robust Policies
proposals.motivation: In partially observable environments, from clinical diagnostics to autonomous navigation, agents must actively gather sensory data to reduce uncertainty. Current exploration methods, like maximizing state entropy, often waste effort on irrelevant information, leading to inefficiency and poor task performance. A principled framework is needed to tie information acquisition directly to the downstream decision quality required for robust, high-reward execution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent which maximizes the mutual information between its planned actions and task-relevant latent state variables, conditioned on its observation policy, will achieve superior task performance with lower perceptual cost compared to standard exploration or information-gathering baselines.
proposals.method: We will formulate the objective as a variational optimization problem within a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP). The core algorithm will integrate a probabilistic world model (e.g., a Bayesian neural network) with a decision tree policy, where the tree's splits represent informative sensing actions. To make the mutual information objective tractable for high-dimensional states, we will leverage recent advances in efficient inner-product search (MIPS) for belief state comparison and employ multi-armed bandit algorithms (inspired by MABSplit) to dynamically allocate computation to the most promising information-gathering paths.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, decision-theoretic objective for active perception that directly links information value to task success. It will provide a scalable algorithmic framework, demonstrated on benchmark robotic manipulation and clinical decision-support datasets, for learning robust policies that are sample- and perception-efficient.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in conditioning the mutual information objective on the agent's planned policy, moving beyond generic uncertainty reduction to task-optimal disambiguation, and in the integration of tractable Bayesian tree priors with bandit-based search for efficient optimization of this objective.

proposals.title: Active Ambiguity-Aware Environment Identification via Multi-Hypothesis Bayesian Forests
proposals.motivation: Robots operating in complex, unstructured environments must infer latent properties (e.g., terrain type, object material) from limited sensorimotor data. This is fundamentally ill-posed, as many distinct environmental models can explain the same passive observations. Current decision-making algorithms, which assume a single best-fit model, become brittle when this assumption fails, leading to poor performance and safety risks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that maintaining a tractable belief distribution over plausible environments using a multi-hypothesis model, and actively planning control sequences to maximally reduce this distribution's entropy, will yield more robust and sample-efficient environment identification than single-model inference or passive data collection.
proposals.method: We will develop a probabilistic multi-hypothesis model where each candidate environment is represented by a Bayesian Decision Tree, leveraging Thrun's prior work on tractable priors. The belief distribution over trees will be updated via sequential Monte Carlo. For active perception, we will use a Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) framework, akin to MABSplit, to efficiently plan informative control actions that maximize expected information gain, measured by the Kullback-Leibler divergence between belief states. The system's output will be a refined set of probable tree models with associated confidence measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, active framework for robust environment identification under ambiguity, directly enabling safer robot decision-making. It will contribute a novel integration of Bayesian non-parametric models with bandit-based active perception, validated on a clinical robotics dataset to demonstrate improved accuracy and calibrated uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop integration of a multi-hypothesis, tree-structured world model with an information-theoretic active control policy, moving beyond passive Bayesian inference to actively resolve perceptual ambiguity through targeted interaction.

proposals.title: Online Bayesian World Model Inference for Robust Dynamic SLAM
proposals.motivation: Current SLAM systems struggle in highly dynamic environments, treating moving objects as outliers or temporary features. For robots to operate reliably long-term in human spaces (e.g., hospitals, warehouses), they must not only map geometry but also understand the generative rules governing object and agent motion. This capability is critical for prediction, planning, and maintaining consistent maps amidst change, a core challenge in pre-2026 mobile robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating dynamic SLAM as a joint online inference problem over the robot's state, the state of dynamic entities, and a latent environmental model (e.g., interaction potentials, motion priors) will yield more accurate, consistent, and predictive maps than systems that treat dynamics as noise.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical probabilistic framework. At the low level, a factor graph SLAM backend will estimate robot pose and object tracks. At the high level, we will infer a compact environmental model, represented as a probabilistic context-free grammar over object interactions or a set of learned potential fields, using online variational inference. Techniques from MABSplit will be adapted to efficiently decide when to expand the model complexity, and fast inner-product search will accelerate matching observed interactions to model components. The inferred model will provide priors for data association and motion prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel SLAM architecture that jointly estimates a generative world model, 2) a concrete methodology for online, incremental learning of environmental dynamics from a single robot's stream of data, and 3) a new benchmark for evaluating long-term consistency and predictive capability in dynamic SLAM.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop, online inference of a generative environmental *cause* of dynamics within the SLAM optimization, moving beyond state estimation to model estimation, creating a self-improving understanding of *why* a scene changes.

proposals.title: ForestDrive: Online Decision Forest Training for Robust Long-Range Navigation with Coarse Maps and Real-Time Sensing
proposals.motivation: Autonomous navigation in unstructured environments (e.g., rural roads, off-road terrain) is critically limited by reliance on high-definition prior maps, which are expensive to create and maintain and fail in dynamic or unmapped regions. A system capable of safe, long-range navigation using only coarse, globally available maps (e.g., OpenStreetMap) and real-time sensor data (camera, lidar) would dramatically increase the operational domain and robustness of autonomous vehicles and robots. This addresses a key pre-2026 challenge in scaling autonomy beyond meticulously pre-mapped urban corridors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an online, multi-modal decision forest, trained continuously on real-time sensor data and weakly guided by coarse map priors, can learn a traversability and navigation policy that outperforms static, HD-map-dependent systems in novel and evolving environments.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tier forest architecture. A fast, lightweight forest (trained via MABSplit for efficiency) will perform real-time pixel/point-wise traversability classification from camera and lidar. Its predictions, along with coarse map features (e.g., road type, shape) and vehicle state, will form the input to a deeper Bayesian Decision Tree ensemble for high-level navigation decision-making (lane selection, speed, stop). This policy forest will be updated online using a probabilistic context-free grammar prior to maintain structured decision logic, with performance evaluated on a clinical dataset of long-range, off-map driving logs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel online learning framework for navigation that fuses coarse symbolic maps with real-time perception, 2) a publicly available benchmark dataset for evaluating HD-map-free navigation performance, and 3) empirical evidence that adaptive tree-based models can serve as a robust, interpretable core for long-term autonomy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop integration of online decision forest training for both perception and control within a navigation stack, moving beyond using forests only for offline classification or static planners, and explicitly encoding the uncertainty of coarse map data via Bayesian tree priors.

proposals.title: Bayesian Forest Bootstrapping: Data-Efficient Terrain Classification via Probabilistic Priors and Multi-Armed Bandit Feature Selection
proposals.motivation: Robust terrain classification is critical for autonomous navigation in unstructured environments, but current supervised models require vast, expensively labeled datasets that are often unavailable for novel or remote terrains. This limits the adaptability and deployment speed of robots and autonomous systems in real-world scenarios like disaster response or planetary exploration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a forest of Bayesian Decision Trees, guided by structured priors over terrain features and trained with an active, bandit-based feature selection strategy, can achieve high classification accuracy with orders of magnitude fewer labeled examples than conventional supervised methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, we encode domain knowledge about terrain geometry and spectral properties as tractable priors within a Bayesian Decision Tree framework, using Probabilistic Context-Free Grammars to define likely tree structures. Second, we adapt the MABSplit method to implement a multi-armed bandit for active feature querying during training, efficiently identifying the most informative sensor modalities (e.g., LiDAR, visual, tactile) for each split with minimal label requests, thereby accelerating forest construction.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, sample-efficient learning algorithm for terrain perception that significantly reduces labeling burden. It will provide a principled framework for incorporating domain knowledge into decision forests and demonstrate a concrete application of bandit-based methods for resource-constrained robotic learning.
proposals.novelty: The integration of structured Bayesian priors for terrain with bandit-driven active feature selection within a decision forest framework is novel, moving beyond standard data augmentation or semi-supervised learning to create a fundamentally more data-efficient and interpretable model class.

proposals.title: Out-of-Distribution Pose Generalization via Bayesian Decision Forests and Learned Skin Deformation Priors
proposals.motivation: Models for human shape and pose estimation, crucial for clinical biomechanics and AR/VR, fail catastrophically when presented with poses far outside their training distribution. This limits their real-world applicability, as human movement is inherently diverse and often extreme. A method to generate plausible deformations for such out-of-distribution (OOD) poses is a critical unsolved problem in articulated 3D reconstruction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid model combining a structured, data-efficient Bayesian decision forest for pose-conditioned reasoning with a learned, physically-inspired prior on skin deformation can generate plausible shapes for extreme OOD poses where purely data-driven neural networks fail.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, a Bayesian Decision Tree forest, building on Thrun's prior work with tractable priors, will partition the pose space and estimate coarse shape parameters, leveraging its strength in uncertainty quantification for OOD detection. Second, for final detailed mesh generation, we will train a neural skinning model regularized by a novel loss derived from a probabilistic context-free grammar (PCFG) that encodes anatomical constraints on bone length and joint limits. The training will use a multi-armed bandit strategy (MABSplit) to efficiently allocate dataset resources between common and rare pose examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a robust framework for OOD pose generalization that explicitly models uncertainty, 2) a new PCFG-based regularizer for anatomically plausible deformations, and 3) a public benchmark dataset with clinically captured extreme poses for evaluating generalization performance beyond standard motion capture ranges.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the principled integration of interpretable, uncertainty-aware Bayesian forests with deep learning for 3D shape, and the formulation of anatomical deformation rules as a learnable PCFG prior, moving beyond simple data interpolation.

proposals.title: Manifold Forests for Efficient and Robust Clinical Pose Estimation
proposals.motivation: Accurate, real-time human pose estimation is critical for clinical applications like physical therapy monitoring and gait analysis, but existing models often struggle with the high-dimensional, continuous nature of pose space, leading to jitter and anatomically implausible predictions. Current deep learning approaches are data-hungry and lack interpretability, while tree-based ensembles, though efficient, fail to capture the intrinsic low-dimensional manifold structure of human movement, limiting their accuracy and robustness in clinical settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating manifold learning directly into the training of decision forest algorithms will yield a more compact, efficient, and physically realistic model of the human pose manifold, significantly improving estimation accuracy and temporal smoothness on limited clinical datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel Manifold Random Forest algorithm. During tree construction, instead of using raw high-dimensional joint coordinates, splits will be optimized on a low-dimensional embedding learned concurrently via a parametric t-SNE or UMAP objective. We will adapt the MABSplit bandit strategy to efficiently explore splits in both the data and latent manifold spaces. Inference will project query poses onto the manifold, ensuring predictions adhere to learned biomechanical constraints, with uncertainty quantified via Bayesian Decision Tree principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new class of interpretable, tree-based models that explicitly learn pose manifolds, 2) a framework for efficient, high-accuracy pose estimation tailored for clinical datasets where labels are scarce, and 3) publicly available benchmarks demonstrating superior performance in smoothness and anatomical plausibility over existing deep and tree-based methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, synergistic integration of manifold learning objectives into the core forest training algorithm itself, moving beyond using manifolds as mere pre/post-processing steps, and specifically optimizing this hybrid for the constraints of clinical performance assessment.

proposals.title: DeepPointForest: Integrating Learned Point Cloud Descriptors with Bayesian Decision Trees for Clinical Skin Lesion Analysis
proposals.motivation: In clinical dermatology, analyzing 3D skin lesion scans from point cloud data relies heavily on hand-crafted voxel features, which may not capture complex morphological patterns critical for accurate diagnosis. This limits the performance of subsequent decision tree models used for their interpretability. A method to automatically learn discriminative, high-dimensional features from raw point clouds could significantly improve diagnostic accuracy for conditions like melanoma.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing hand-crafted voxel features with descriptors learned by a point cloud neural network will yield more informative representations, leading to improved accuracy and robustness of a downstream Bayesian decision forest model for skin lesion classification.
proposals.method: We will employ a PointNet++ architecture to process raw 3D skin lesion point clouds and extract a global feature descriptor. This learned descriptor will serve as the input feature vector for a forest of Bayesian Decision Trees, trained using tractable priors and probabilistic grammars as in Thrun's prior work. We will optimize the pipeline end-to-end, using multi-armed bandit strategies (MABSplit) to accelerate forest training on the high-dimensional learned features and enable efficient maximum inner product search during inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel hybrid architecture that combines the representational power of deep learning with the interpretable, probabilistic reasoning of decision trees for clinical 3D data. It will provide a new benchmark for skin lesion analysis from point clouds and a general framework for replacing engineered features in medical decision forests.
proposals.novelty: While deep learning for point clouds and decision trees are mature fields, their tight integration for clinical skin analysis is novel, specifically the use of a PointNet++ feature extractor optimized for a Bayesian forest backend, moving beyond simple voxel-grid approximations.

proposals.title: Dual-Registration Forests: Voxel Feature Compression via Informative Subspace Learning for Clinical Image Alignment
proposals.motivation: High-resolution 3D medical images (e.g., CT, MRI) are data-intensive, creating storage and transmission bottlenecks in clinical workflows. Current compression methods are generic and can degrade features critical for downstream tasks like deformable image registration, which is essential for longitudinal studies and image-guided surgery. A method that compresses data by preserving only the voxel features most informative for registration would enable efficient, task-aware data handling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a registration-driven, decision forest-based feature selection framework can identify a highly compressed, task-specific subspace of voxel features that maintains registration accuracy comparable to using the full data.
proposals.method: We will train a multi-task random forest where one objective is to predict a deformation field for registration, and a parallel, sparsity-inducing objective selects a minimal set of discriminative voxel features. Inspired by MABSplit, we will use a multi-armed bandit strategy to efficiently explore the high-dimensional voxel feature space during tree construction. The framework will be trained end-to-end, using a probabilistic prior over feature subsets (informed by Bayesian Decision Trees) to guide the search towards compressible, informative features.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel algorithm for task-aware compression of volumetric medical data, directly optimizing the trade-off between compression ratio and registration fidelity. It will provide a new interpretable tool for understanding which image features are most salient for alignment, with potential applications in federated learning and edge computing for clinical datasets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing voxel compression as a feature selection problem directly optimized within the registration task itself, using advanced forest training techniques (MAB, Bayesian priors) to make the high-dimensional search tractable, a departure from generic compression or hand-crafted feature methods.

proposals.title: Data-Frugal Self-Training via Bayesian Decision Trees and Multi-Armed Bandit Data Selection
proposals.motivation: Self-training and semi-supervised learning typically assume a large pool of unlabeled data, which is unavailable in many critical domains like clinical diagnostics or rare document analysis. In these scenarios, even unlabeled documents are scarce, creating a 'data desert' that renders standard self-training ineffective. This work addresses the fundamental challenge of learning robust models when both labeled and unlabeled data are extremely limited, a common and understudied bottleneck in real-world AI deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-training framework, which integrates a strong, data-efficient prior via Bayesian Decision Trees and actively optimizes the selection of pseudo-labels using a Multi-Armed Bandit (MAB) strategy, can achieve high accuracy and robustness even when the available unlabeled data is orders of magnitude smaller than typical benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel iterative self-training algorithm. The core learner will be a Bayesian Decision Tree, leveraging tractable priors to prevent overfitting on small labeled sets. In each iteration, the limited unlabeled pool will be treated as a set of 'arms' for a contextual MAB (inspired by MABSplit), where the reward is the expected information gain or model confidence change from pseudo-labeling a document. This MAB module will actively and efficiently select the most informative subset of unlabeled points for pseudo-labeling, maximizing the utility of every scarce data point. The inner product search component will be adapted for efficient similarity-based reward estimation within the bandit's context.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, practical framework for semi-supervised learning in data-scarce environments, directly applicable to clinical and archival settings. It will advance the theoretical understanding of data selection in self-training by formalizing it as a sequential decision problem under extreme uncertainty. We will release a benchmark for 'data-desert' learning and an open-source implementation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight integration of a bandit-based active selection mechanism directly into the self-training loop specifically for scarcity, moving beyond passive sampling. This approach uniquely combines the uncertainty quantification of Bayesian Decision Trees with the efficient exploration-exploitation trade-off of MABs to tackle the extreme low-data regime.

proposals.title: End-to-End Forest Learning: A Unified Probabilistic Framework for Seed-Guided and Unsupervised Data Partitioning
proposals.motivation: In clinical and skin cancer diagnostics, a common pipeline uses a two-module approach: first, an unsupervised clustering or embedding on unlabeled data, followed by a supervised decision tree/forest trained on a small seed dataset. This sequential process creates a suboptimal information flow, where the final classifier cannot refine the initial data partitioning. An end-to-end model that jointly optimizes both stages could significantly improve accuracy and data efficiency, crucial for domains with limited labeled data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating the structure learning of a data partition with the discriminative learning of a decision forest into a single, coherent probabilistic model will yield superior performance and more data-efficient learning compared to the sequential pipeline, especially when labeled seeds are scarce.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified graphical model combining a Bayesian decision tree prior, inspired by probabilistic context-free grammars, with a latent variable model for the unlabeled data. The model will perform joint inference over latent cluster assignments (informed by both data density and seed labels) and tree parameters. Training will leverage stochastic variational inference, with efficient search over tree structures guided by a bandit-based method (MABSplit) adapted to balance exploration of the data space and exploitation of seed labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, tractable algorithm for end-to-end forest training from mixed labeled and unlabeled data. It will provide a theoretical framework for joint structure and parameter learning, and deliver empirical validation on clinical and dermatological datasets, demonstrating gains in accuracy and label efficiency over state-of-the-art sequential methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fully integrated probabilistic formulation, which moves beyond using unsupervised results as fixed inputs, instead allowing bidirectional interaction between data-driven partitioning and supervised decision boundaries within a single training objective.

proposals.title: Multi-Label Pseudo-Document Self-Training for Scalable Clinical Text Classification
proposals.motivation: Multi-label classification of clinical documents (e.g., assigning multiple diagnostic codes to a single patient note) is a critical but challenging task due to label correlations and data scarcity. Pre-2026 methods like pseudo-document generation and self-training have shown promise for single-label text tasks, but their direct application to multi-label settings is underexplored. Extending these techniques could significantly improve the performance and data efficiency of automated clinical coding systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-training framework, enhanced with a multi-label-aware pseudo-document generation mechanism based on probabilistic context-free grammars, can effectively leverage unlabeled clinical text to improve the accuracy and robustness of multi-label decision tree ensembles.
proposals.method: We will extend the pseudo-document generation framework to model label co-occurrence statistics, using a Bayesian prior derived from the training set to guide the generation of synthetic documents with multiple, correlated labels. This will be integrated into a self-training loop where an ensemble of decision trees (trained via methods like MABSplit for efficiency) iteratively labels a large unlabeled corpus. We will employ a multi-label sampling strategy for the pseudo-documents, informed by the ensemble's confidence estimates, to refine the model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable method for multi-label text classification that reduces reliance on large, fully-annotated datasets. It will deliver a publicly available benchmark for multi-label clinical text classification and demonstrate how tree-based models can be competitively applied to complex, high-dimensional text tasks with structured outputs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of generative, multi-label structured priors with efficient tree ensemble training within a self-training loop, specifically tailored for the statistical dependencies inherent in clinical multi-label problems, moving beyond simple binary relevance assumptions.

proposals.title: Generalizing Weakly-Supervised Priors: A Bayesian Framework for Efficient Training of Diverse Neural Architectures
proposals.motivation: Weakly-supervised learning is crucial for domains like medical imaging (e.g., skin lesion analysis) where obtaining pixel-perfect annotations is costly. While recent work has developed promising modules for specific CNNs, their applicability to other architectures like Vision Transformers (ViTs), Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), and efficient MobileNets remains unexplored.
