This limits the framework's impact and prevents understanding its general principles for data-efficient training.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core Bayesian principles and tractable priors from recent decision tree and forest work can be abstracted into a portable, architecture-agnostic module that injects structured, weakly-supervised knowledge, thereby consistently improving sample efficiency and robustness across diverse neural models without sacrificing inference speed.
proposals.method: We will abstract the weakly-supervised framework into a plug-in 'prior module' defined by a probabilistic context-free grammar, similar to the Bayesian Decision Tree prior work. This module will be integrated via attention mechanisms in ViTs, message-passing layers in GNNs, and efficient depthwise convolutions in MobileNets. Training will leverage multi-armed bandit strategies (MABSplit) for adaptive resource allocation during the co-training of the prior and the base network, and we will use maximum inner product search (MIPS) techniques for efficient inference of the prior's latent states.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, general-purpose toolkit for weakly-supervised learning applicable to a broad class of modern architectures, significantly expanding the framework's utility. It will provide empirical benchmarks on clinical and standard vision datasets, demonstrating gains in accuracy and training efficiency. The study will also yield theoretical insights into what architectural properties enable effective integration of structured Bayesian priors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in decoupling the weakly-supervised framework from a specific model family and formalizing it as a composable, architecture-agnostic prior, a step not taken in prior CNN-specific applications. Furthermore, integrating bandit-based training and MIPS for this generalized case is unexplored.

proposals.title: Cross-Domain Robustness of Bayesian Decision Forests for Text Classification: A Benchmark on Specialized Corpora
proposals.motivation: While decision forests and their Bayesian variants have shown strong performance on standard text benchmarks, their effectiveness across highly specialized, domain-specific corpora remains underexplored. In fields like clinical medicine, legal analysis, and technical engineering, where data is sparse and terminology is niche, the generalization of efficient, interpretable models is critical for real-world deployment. A systematic evaluation across diverse domains is necessary to move beyond generic benchmarks and understand where these methods truly excel or require adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Bayesian decision forests, leveraging tractable priors and efficient search techniques, will demonstrate superior robustness and consistent high accuracy compared to standard deep learning baselines when evaluated across a wide array of specialized text classification domains, particularly in low-data regimes.
proposals.method: We will implement a Bayesian Decision Tree framework using Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar priors for structure learning. For training efficiency on high-dimensional text features, we will integrate the MABSplit algorithm for optimal split selection and employ fast maximum inner product search (MIPS) techniques for nearest-neighbor computations within ensemble methods. The method will be benchmarked against standard neural models (e.g., LSTMs, simple transformers) on a newly curated dataset comprising text from at least 10 specialized domains (e.g., clinical notes, patent texts, scientific abstracts, legal contracts), measuring accuracy, calibration, and training time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, cross-domain performance analysis of modern Bayesian forest methods for text, establishing clear guidelines for their application in specialized fields. It will yield a public benchmark of curated, domain-specific text datasets to foster future research in robust, interpretable NLP.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in a single new algorithm, but in the rigorous, multi-domain experimental design that tests the limits of current Bayesian forest methods, explicitly connecting algorithmic innovations in efficient training (MABSplit, MIPS) to practical performance across real-world, specialized corpora where interpretability and data efficiency are paramount.

proposals.title: ProtoTreeShot: Prototypical Networks for Few-Shot Decision Tree Induction in Clinical Skin Cancer Datasets
proposals.motivation: In clinical dermatology, acquiring large, expertly labeled datasets for rare skin conditions is a significant bottleneck, limiting the performance and applicability of automated diagnostic tools. While decision forests offer interpretability, their standard training requires abundant data. Few-shot learning, particularly prototypical networks, has shown success in vision tasks with minimal examples, but its principles have not been systematically integrated into the core induction algorithms of interpretable tree-based models for clinical use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding prototypical network representations into the node-splitting criterion of a decision tree will enable the induction of more accurate and data-efficient models from few labeled examples in a clinical skin lesion dataset, without sacrificing interpretability.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel tree induction algorithm where, at each node, candidate splits are evaluated not only on feature thresholds but also on their ability to separate learned prototype embeddings of the support (few-shot) examples. We will leverage a pre-trained convolutional network (e.g., on ImageNet) to generate initial embeddings for dermoscopic images. A prototypical loss will be integrated into the Bayesian framework from prior work, creating a tractable prior that favors splits aligning with the few-shot class prototypes. The MABSplit technique will be adapted to efficiently explore this enriched split space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, hybrid model class that combines the data efficiency of metric-based meta-learning with the interpretable decision structure of trees, specifically validated on a clinical skin cancer benchmark (e.g., HAM10000). It will provide a practical algorithm for building diagnostic aids in low-data regimes. The code and evaluation framework will be released to the community.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct fusion of prototypical network objectives into the tree-splitting mechanism itself, moving beyond using meta-learning merely as a pre-processing step. This creates an inductive bias fundamentally shaped by few-shot learning, which is non-obvious given that tree algorithms traditionally rely solely on local, greedy optimization of impurity metrics.

proposals.title: MABStream: Efficient Self-Training for Web-Scale Data via Multi-Armed Bandits and Streaming Decision Forests
proposals.motivation: The success of deep learning is increasingly dependent on massive, often unlabeled, web-scale corpora. However, the standard self-training loop, which iteratively labels data with a teacher model and retrains a student, is prohibitively expensive and slow at this scale, struggling with data selection and catastrophic forgetting. Efficiently scaling this process is a critical bottleneck for advancing model performance in data-rich but label-poor domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-training framework combining streaming decision forests for efficient online learning with multi-armed bandit (MAB) policies for adaptive data selection can dramatically improve the efficiency and final accuracy of models trained on web-scale unlabeled streams.
proposals.method: We will develop MABStream, a system where a lightweight streaming decision forest (inspired by MABSplit) acts as the rapidly updating student model. A MAB policy will govern the sampling of data from the unlabeled stream, framing each potential data batch as an 'arm' and using the forest's performance gain on a held-out validation set as the reward to prioritize high-utility examples. The teacher model, a larger neural network, will be updated less frequently on high-confidence pseudo-labels from the forest, creating a computationally efficient loop. We will leverage fast maximum inner product search for nearest-neighbor operations within the forest.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable algorithm for semi-supervised learning on unbounded data streams, significantly reducing computational cost compared to iterative full-dataset training. It will also contribute a principled, adaptive data selection mechanism for self-training, moving beyond simple confidence thresholding. The resulting system will be validated on large-scale clinical and text datasets to demonstrate gains in accuracy and training speed.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic integration of bandit-based active sampling with streaming tree ensembles for the self-training paradigm, a combination not explored in pre-2026 literature. This moves beyond static dataset sampling to a dynamic, reward-driven data acquisition strategy optimized for continual learning.

proposals.title: Meta-LabelNet: A Noise-Aware Meta-Learning Framework for Robust Decision Trees from Crowd-Sourced Clinical Data
proposals.motivation: Crowd-sourcing enables the rapid collection of large-scale labels for clinical datasets, such as skin lesion annotations, but introduces significant label noise and bias from heterogeneous annotator expertise. Pre-2026, standard methods like expectation-maximization or simple weighting struggle with complex, non-stationary noise patterns, degrading model performance and reliability. A principled approach to deconvolve annotator behavior and recover a clean training signal is critical for building trustworthy diagnostic algorithms from noisy real-world data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the crowd-sourcing process explicitly through a meta-learner, which estimates per-annotator reliability and systematic biases in an online fashion, will produce a refined training signal that yields more robust and accurate primary models, particularly decision trees and forests, compared to training directly on aggregated noisy labels.
proposals.method: We propose a bi-level optimization framework. The primary model is a decision tree ensemble (e.g., a forest trained via MABSplit for efficiency). The meta-learner is a probabilistic model, inspired by Bayesian Decision Trees and PCFG priors, that treats each annotator's label as a function of a latent true label and annotator-specific parameters (competence, bias). The meta-learner updates these parameters via online inference, and its output—a probabilistic consensus—trains the primary model. We will leverage fast inner-product search for efficient similarity matching between examples and annotator profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel meta-learning algorithm for label denoising that explicitly models annotator populations, a scalable implementation integrated with fast tree training methods, and a rigorous empirical evaluation on public clinical datasets with simulated and real crowd-sourced labels, demonstrating improved accuracy and robustness.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on label noise, our method reframes the problem as a dynamic meta-learning task, jointly inferring a latent truth and a structured model of the annotation process, rather than applying static corrections or relying solely on aggregated labels.

proposals.title: CrowdForest: Bootstrapping High-Accuracy Decision Trees from Noisy Crowdsourced Labels via Iterative Refinement
proposals.motivation: In many clinical and real-world domains, acquiring expert-labeled datasets for supervised learning is prohibitively expensive and slow, creating a major bottleneck for model development. While crowdsourcing platforms offer a scalable source of labels, the resulting data is inherently noisy and unreliable for training high-performance models. This work addresses the critical challenge of bootstrapping expert-level model accuracy from an initial, cheap set of weak, crowdsourced annotations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an active learning loop, combining a probabilistic label aggregation model with a decision forest trained on progressively refined consensus labels, can iteratively identify the most uncertain examples for re-labeling by the crowd, enabling the system to converge to expert-level performance without initial expert data.
proposals.method: We will develop an online variant of the Dawid-Skene estimator to continuously aggregate incoming crowd labels into probabilistic training targets. A decision forest (leveraging methods like MABSplit for efficient training) will be trained on these targets. Its prediction entropy will guide an active learning policy to select the most uncertain instances for additional crowd labeling. This iterative loop of model training, uncertainty sampling, label gathering, and consensus refinement will be repeated until performance plateaus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for building high-accuracy models in label-scarce domains using only crowdsourcing, significantly reducing dependency on experts. It will also contribute a novel active learning strategy tailored for learning from multiple noisy annotators, advancing the field of data-efficient machine learning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop, bootstrapping integration of continuous statistical consensus modeling with decision forest training, inverting the standard paradigm by starting with noisy crowdsourced data and actively refining it towards expert quality, rather than using experts to clean data first.

proposals.title: Swarm-GPS: Bootstrapping Collective Localization via Multi-Armed Bandit Forest Training on Weakly Supervised Consensus
proposals.motivation: Micro-robot swarms for exploration or medical applications operate with severe sensor and compute constraints, making traditional SLAM or GPS-based localization impossible. Pre-programming models for unknown, complex environments is infeasible, and collecting expert-labeled ground truth for training is impractical at scale. A method for swarms to autonomously learn localization from their own noisy, collective observations is a critical unsolved problem for real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A swarm's inherent spatial redundancy and temporal consensus can generate a sufficient supervisory signal for training robust, lightweight decision forest models for individual robot localization, creating a system where the collective model outperforms any pre-designed model or individual robot's estimate.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as weakly supervised learning where training targets are derived from inter-robot proximity observations and motion parallax, forming a probabilistic consistency graph. A core technical innovation will be adapting the MABSplit algorithm to efficiently train an ensemble of decision trees (a forest) where each tree predicts a robot's latent state. Multi-armed bandits will optimally allocate computational effort to split decisions that maximize the consistency of the emerging swarm-wide map, using techniques from Bayesian Decision Trees to incorporate prior knowledge about robot dynamics and sensor noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable algorithm for swarm self-localization that requires no external infrastructure or pre-labeled data. It will advance the field of weakly supervised robotics by providing a concrete framework for turning swarm consensus into a training objective, and will demonstrate significant performance gains in accuracy and training speed over static models on simulated and real micro-robot datasets.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses consensus for state estimation, using it as a direct, online training signal for a machine learning model is novel. The fusion of MABSplit for efficient forest training with a weakly supervised objective derived from swarm physics creates a new paradigm for adaptive algorithm design in robot collectives.

proposals.title: Bandit-Guided Forest Training with Noisy Crowd-Sourced Labels via Probabilistic Tree Priors
proposals.motivation: Expert-curated datasets are a major bottleneck in scaling machine learning for clinical and specialized domains, limiting the size and diversity of training data. While crowd-sourcing offers a scalable alternative, the resulting noisy, weakly-labeled data severely degrades the performance of high-stakes decision models like random forests. This work addresses the critical need for algorithms that can learn robustly from imperfect, non-expert annotations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a probabilistic model of label noise directly into the tree induction process and using multi-armed bandits for efficient split exploration, we can train decision forests whose performance on expert-validated test sets matches or exceeds that of forests trained on costly expert data alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the Bayesian Decision Tree framework to incorporate a latent variable model for crowd-sourced labels, using a tractable prior over tree structures to regularize learning. The MABSplit algorithm will be adapted to optimize splits not for raw label purity, but for inferred 'true' label consistency, accelerating training in high-dimensional feature spaces. Validation will use clinical datasets with both expert and crowd-sourced annotations, benchmarking against standard forests and label aggregation baselines.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a novel, scalable training algorithm for decision forests that inverts the expert dependency, enabling high-accuracy models from cheap, noisy data. It contributes a principled Bayesian bandit method for learning with weak supervision, directly applicable to medical diagnosis and other label-constrained domains.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that cleans labels before training or uses simple aggregation, our method jointly learns the forest structure and the latent true label distribution, with bandits making this joint inference computationally tractable for large-scale problems.

proposals.title: Federated Forests for Dermatology: A Bandit-Driven Framework with Targeted Synthetic Augmentation for Equitable Skin Lesion Classification
proposals.motivation: Current AI models for dermatology suffer from poor generalizability due to training on non-representative, centralized datasets, leading to significant performance disparities across skin tones. This inequity limits clinical utility and raises ethical concerns. Federated learning (FL) offers a path to diverse data, but standard FL for image classification is inefficient and struggles with severe class and demographic imbalances inherent in distributed clinical data.
proposals.hypothesis: A federated learning system that integrates efficient decision forest training with multi-armed bandits for client selection and strategically augments local datasets with condition- and phenotype-specific synthetic images will produce dermatology AI models with significantly improved and more equitable accuracy across diverse populations.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel FL framework where client nodes train Bayesian Decision Trees, leveraging their probabilistic nature for effective aggregation. Inspired by MABSplit, we will employ a multi-armed bandit algorithm to dynamically select clients for each round, prioritizing those with data distributions that most improve global model performance on held-out validation sets representing diverse skin tones. Locally, we will use state-of-the-art generative models (e.g., diffusion models) to create synthetic dermatology images, guided by a gap analysis of the federated metadata, to specifically augment under-represented conditions and Fitzpatrick skin types before local training.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, privacy-preserving framework for building more equitable dermatology AI. It will provide new algorithms for efficient federated training of tree-based models and a validated methodology for using synthetic data to address specific real-world diversity gaps. The resulting model will be benchmarked for fairness across skin tones on diverse test sets.
proposals.novelty: The integration of bandit-optimized client selection with Bayesian Decision Trees in FL is novel, as is the targeted, metadata-driven use of synthetic data specifically to correct for demographic imbalances within a federated system, moving beyond simple dataset scaling.

proposals.title: Federated Forest SLAM: Building Demographic-Invariant Person Models via Distributed Sensor Networks
proposals.motivation: Computer vision and sensing systems often fail to generalize across diverse demographics due to biased training datasets. Simultaneously, deploying robust, large-scale sensor networks for applications like assisted living or public space monitoring requires models that perform accurately and fairly for all individuals. Current methods for creating diverse datasets are costly and static, while SLAM systems primarily focus on static geometry, ignoring the opportunity to learn from the dynamic human data they observe.
proposals.hypothesis: A static, multi-modal sensor network can use a distributed SLAM framework to jointly map an environment and build a continuously growing, in-situ dataset of human appearances, enabling the training of embedded models that are invariant to demographic attributes through federated self-supervision.
proposals.method: We will deploy a network of RGB-D and RF sensors in a complex indoor environment. A core technical innovation is treating people as moving landmarks within a distributed, factor graph-based SLAM backend, fusing observations across modalities. For model training, we adapt a federated learning framework where each node uses local observations to train a decision forest-based embedding model, leveraging techniques like MABSplit for efficient forest training. The global objective will use a contrastive loss to maximize consistency of embeddings for the same person across views and conditions while minimizing correlation with protected demographic attributes inferred via Bayesian decision tree priors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel distributed SLAM formulation that explicitly models and learns from human dynamics, 2) a method for generating massive, realistic, and continuously improving demographic-diverse datasets in-situ, and 3) a federated training framework for producing demographic-invariant person representation models that improve edge network performance and fairness.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of distributed SLAM as a data engine for federated fairness training is novel. Unlike prior work, the system does not require a pre-collected dataset; it creates and learns from its own data stream, explicitly optimizing for demographic invariance within the constraints of an embedded sensor network.

proposals.title: Dynamic Contextual Fusion for Robust Medical Diagnosis: A Sensor-Augmented Neural Network with Bayesian Decision Tree Priors
proposals.motivation: Clinical diagnosis often relies on multi-modal data, such as dermoscopic images and patient metadata for skin cancer screening. Current deep learning models typically use simple early concatenation of features, which fails to account for the varying reliability and contextual relevance of each modality, potentially limiting diagnostic accuracy and robustness. Drawing inspiration from robust sensor fusion in autonomous vehicles, a principled, adaptive fusion method is needed for clinical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network architecture that dynamically weights the contribution of different diagnostic data modalities based on learned contextual confidence will yield higher diagnostic accuracy and robustness compared to static fusion baselines. Incorporating structured, interpretable priors from Bayesian decision trees into the fusion process will further enhance model reliability.
proposals.method: We propose a late-fusion neural network with parallel streams: a CNN for image data and an MLP for tabular metadata. Inspired by autonomous vehicle sensor fusion, a central gating network, implemented as a small neural network or a differentiable tree-based model, will learn to output context-dependent weights for each modality's feature vector before final classification. To ground this in Thrun's recent work, we will explore using a tractable Bayesian Decision Tree prior, informed by a Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar, to regularize the gating network's structure, promoting interpretability. Training will leverage clinical datasets with performance evaluated via accuracy and calibration metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, context-aware fusion architecture for medical AI that moves beyond simple feature concatenation. It will provide a framework for integrating interpretable, tree-based priors into deep neural networks for clinical decision-making, potentially improving both performance and trust. The resulting model will be a step towards more robust, multi-modal diagnostic systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct adaptation of dynamic sensor-fusion principles from autonomous systems to clinical diagnosis, combined with the integration of recent advances in Bayesian Decision Trees to provide structured, interpretable priors for the fusion mechanism, which is not present in standard multi-modal deep learning.

proposals.title: MABSplit-Health: A Federated Edge Architecture for Low-Latency Clinical Decision Support on Mobile Devices
proposals.motivation: Real-time clinical diagnostics at the point-of-care are hampered by network latency and connectivity issues, especially in resource-constrained or mobile settings. Existing cloud-dependent mobile health tools introduce dangerous delays for time-sensitive decisions like arrhythmia detection or sepsis risk stratification. An edge computing paradigm, proven for split-second decisions in autonomous vehicles, is urgently needed to bring reliable, instantaneous inference to frontline clinicians.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid edge-cloud architecture, using lightweight yet high-accuracy decision forests optimized for on-device execution, can deliver clinical-grade diagnostic performance with sub-second latency, independent of network quality, while enabling secure, federated model updates from distributed patient data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MABSplit algorithm for fast, data-efficient training of compact decision tree ensembles deployable on mobile hardware. A Bayesian Decision Tree framework will provide uncertainty estimates for on-edge predictions. The core on-device model will perform initial inference, while a cloud backend handles complex retraining using a federated learning scheme across institutions. We will implement a secure sync protocol, inspired by high-dimensional similarity search for efficient model delta updates, and validate the system on a clinical dataset of ECG waveforms and dermatology images.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source software architecture for low-latency mobile clinical decision support. It will advance methods for creating ultra-efficient diagnostic models for edge deployment and demonstrate a practical federated learning pipeline for healthcare that preserves data privacy. The resulting system will be rigorously validated for accuracy and latency against cloud-only baselines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the principled co-design of a high-speed forest training algorithm (MABSplit) with a Bayesian uncertainty-quantifying prior for robust edge clinical inference, and its integration into a federated edge-cloud health system—a direct translation of autonomous vehicle computational paradigms to a critically underserved clinical need.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Bayesian Nonparametric Landmark Models for Interpretable Few-Shot Diagnosis of Rare Dermatological Conditions
proposals.motivation: Deep learning for dermatology struggles with rare skin lesions due to scarce annotated data. While few-shot meta-learning (e.g., MAML) offers adaptation, it lacks interpretability and assumes fixed model structures. For clinical trust and actionable insights, models must explain decisions by identifying and adapting to lesion-specific anatomical features, a task where annotated landmarks for rare conditions are nonexistent.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework that learns a prior over the parameters of a Bayesian nonparametric landmark discovery model will enable rapid, few-shot adaptation to new skin conditions while simultaneously inferring a data-driven, variable number of semantically meaningful visual landmarks, improving both diagnostic accuracy and model interpretability.
proposals.method: We will develop a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) outer loop that learns an initialization for an inner-loop landmark discovery network. The inner model will be a convolutional neural network whose final layer is a Bayesian nonparametric process, such as a Mondrian Process or a dependent Dirichlet Process, to partition the lesion's feature map into a variable number of landmark regions. Adaptation on a few support images of a new condition will involve fine-tuning the network and sampling from the posterior over landmark configurations using stochastic variational inference, with the meta-learned prior regularizing this inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel, interpretable framework for few-shot medical image analysis that discovers visual concepts without landmark supervision. It will provide clinicians with a system that not only classifies rare lesions but also highlights and adapts the salient anatomical features driving the decision, bridging the gap between high performance and explainable AI in dermatology.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of gradient-based meta-learning with Bayesian nonparametric spatial models for dynamic, task-dependent landmark discovery is novel. Unlike prior fixed-architecture meta-learners or supervised landmark detectors, this approach jointly learns to rapidly adapt and to explain its adaptation through inferred, data-driven visual concepts.

proposals.title: Bayesian Deep Forests: Uncertainty-Aware Decision Trees via Dropout Ensembles and Dirichlet Priors
proposals.motivation: In high-stakes clinical applications, such as skin lesion classification from images, model accuracy alone is insufficient; reliable uncertainty estimates are critical for safe deployment. While Bayesian deep learning offers uncertainty quantification, it is often computationally heavy and opaque. Decision forests, favored for their interpretability and strong performance on tabular and structured data, lack principled, efficient uncertainty measures, limiting their trustworthiness in medical decision support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating scalable Bayesian deep learning techniques, specifically Monte Carlo Dropout and Dirichlet prior networks, into the forest training and inference process, we can equip tree ensembles with accurate, instance-specific uncertainty estimates without sacrificing their training speed or decision-path interpretability.
proposals.method: We will extend the MABSplit framework to efficiently train an ensemble of trees where each tree is treated as a member of a Bayesian model. During inference, we will apply Monte Carlo Dropout at the node level, performing multiple stochastic passes to generate a predictive distribution. Concurrently, we will adapt the Dirichlet Prior Network approach to calibrate the ensemble's class probabilities, modeling epistemic uncertainty in the leaf nodes. The final uncertainty score will combine predictive entropy and the Dirichlet concentration parameters to flag low-confidence cases.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a fast, scalable method for uncertainty quantification in decision forests, directly applicable to clinical datasets. It will provide a practical tool for human-in-the-loop systems, where low-confidence predictions are routed for expert review, enhancing the safety and trustworthiness of automated diagnostic aids.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of tractable Bayesian deep learning principles with the forest training algorithms pioneered by Thrun's group (e.g., MABSplit, Bayesian Decision Trees), creating a hybrid that retains the speed of forests while gaining the uncertainty calibration of deep Bayesian models, a combination not yet fully explored.

proposals.title: MABAL: Multi-Armed Bandit Active Learning for Efficient Clinical Dermatology Dataset Curation
proposals.motivation: In clinical dermatology, building large, accurately labeled image datasets for training diagnostic AI is a major bottleneck due to the high cost and time required from medical experts. Current active learning strategies often rely on computationally expensive uncertainty sampling or diversity measures that scale poorly with large, high-dimensional image data, limiting their practical utility in real-world medical data pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing the image selection problem in active learning as a multi-armed bandit (MAB) problem will yield a more computationally efficient and performance-optimal strategy for growing clinical skin lesion datasets than standard uncertainty-based query strategies.
proposals.method: We will develop MABAL, a method that treats each unlabeled image in a pool as an arm in a bandit problem. The reward for labeling an arm is defined as the expected reduction in overall model uncertainty, approximated using a committee of lightweight Bayesian decision trees. We will adapt the MABSplit algorithm's bandit framework for efficient arm selection to rapidly identify the most informative batches of images for expert labeling, integrating it with a scalable maximum inner product search (MIPS) for similarity-based deduplication in the feature space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable active learning algorithm specifically tailored for high-dimensional medical imaging. It will provide an open-source framework for efficient clinical dataset curation and demonstrate a significant reduction in labeling effort required to achieve state-of-the-art diagnostic accuracy on benchmark dermatology datasets like ISIC.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the unique fusion of multi-armed bandits for sample selection with Bayesian decision tree ensembles for uncertainty estimation, creating a highly efficient and interpretable active learning loop specifically for the clinical domain, moving beyond generic deep learning uncertainty methods.

proposals.title: Real-Time Skin Lesion Analysis via Bandit-Optimized Decision Forests and Efficient GANs on Embedded Hardware
proposals.motivation: Real-time, on-device diagnostic support for dermatological conditions requires high-accuracy models that operate under strict computational and latency constraints. Current deep learning approaches, including GANs for data augmentation and complex ensembles, are too resource-intensive for embedded medical devices, limiting their clinical utility at the point of care. Optimizing these systems for efficiency without sacrificing diagnostic performance is a critical unsolved problem in mobile health.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating multi-armed bandit algorithms for efficient forest construction with a streamlined Monte Carlo GAN architecture, we can achieve real-time inference speeds on embedded hardware while maintaining clinical-grade accuracy on skin lesion datasets.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MABSplit algorithm to accelerate the training of Bayesian decision tree ensembles, optimizing split evaluation for resource-constrained hardware. For the generative component, we will develop a lightweight GAN using techniques like knowledge distillation and efficient inner product search (based on Thrun's prior MIPS work) to accelerate the Monte Carlo sampling process for synthetic sample generation. The integrated system will be validated on clinical dermatology datasets (e.g., HAM10000), benchmarking accuracy, latency, and power consumption against state-of-the-art models on embedded platforms like the NVIDIA Jetson.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, co-optimized machine learning pipeline for real-time skin lesion analysis that is deployable on embedded hardware. It will provide a new benchmark for the trade-off between computational efficiency and diagnostic accuracy in point-of-care clinical settings. The released models and optimization frameworks will enable broader adoption of AI-assisted dermatology in resource-limited environments.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of bandit-optimized decision forest training with a resource-conscious Monte Carlo GAN architecture represents a significant departure from separately optimizing these components. This holistic system-level optimization for a specific clinical embedded task is not addressed in existing literature.

ideas.title: Operate with extremely limited unlabeled data
ideas.description: Adapt the self-training module for scenarios where even unlabeled documents are scarce.
ideas.sourceTitle: Text Classification from Labeled and Unlabeled Documents using EM

ideas.title: Scale to massive unlabeled corpora efficiently
ideas.description: Optimize the self-training loop for web-scale unlabeled data with streaming or sampling.
ideas.sourceTitle: Text Classification from Labeled and Unlabeled Documents using EM

ideas.title: Alias-Robust Probabilistic Data Association with Hierarchical World Hypotheses
ideas.description: This approach develops methods for probabilistic data association that are robust to severe perceptual aliasing and ambiguity. It uses a multi-stage process where cheap, non-parametric tests (e.g., topology, viewpoint geometry) generate and prune a set of plausible global data association hypotheses. Each surviving hypothesis instantiates a separate probabilistic filter or smoothing model, allowing the system to maintain parallel interpretations of the environment. The system finally employs an entropy-based criterion to either merge these world hypotheses when uncertainty is reduced or to actively seek disambiguating information through targeted exploration.

ideas.title: Develop more robust machine learning for terrain
ideas.description: Create learning algorithms less dependent on large, manually-labeled training datasets for terrain classification.
ideas.sourceTitle: Stanley: The robot that won the DARPA Grand Challenge

ideas.title: Crowdsourced Consensus via Iterative Refinement and Aggregation
ideas.description: This idea inverts the expert dependency by adapting the solution from the astronomy field's Galaxy Zoo project. In Galaxy Zoo, the massive task of classifying galaxy morphologies was solved by crowdsourcing to online volunteers and using statistical consensus models to aggregate multiple noisy labels into reliable data. Adapted to ML training, we propose a system that starts with weakly labeled or crowdsourced data and employs an active, iterative loop: the model's predictions identify uncertain examples, which are sent for additional crowd labeling. A continuous statistical aggregation process (like an online Dawid-Skene estimator) refines the training targets from this dynamic influx of weak labels, enabling the model to bootstrap its own supervision and converge towards expert-level performance without initial expert-curated data.

ideas.title: Challenge the two-module sequential pipeline
ideas.description: Explore a fully integrated, end-to-end model that jointly learns from seeds and unlabeled data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Text Classification from Labeled and Unlabeled Documents using EM

ideas.title: Inverse Online Environment Model Inference for Dynamic SLAM
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel SLAM framework that treats the dynamic environment as an output of a hidden generative process. By applying inverse problem methodologies to real-time sensor data and robot trajectory, the system simultaneously estimates the current state of dynamic objects and infers the underlying environmental model—such as physical rules, interaction potentials, or agent intentions—that explains their motion. This inferred model is then used to predict future scene configurations and disambiguate observations, significantly improving robustness and long-term consistency in highly non-stationary settings. The result is an online, self-improving world model that moves beyond geometric mapping to reason about the causes of change within the environment.

ideas.title: Importance sampling proposals from optimization
ideas.description: Use local optimization scans to generate better proposal distributions for importance sampling.
ideas.sourceTitle: Monte Carlo Localization: Efficient Position Estimation for Mobile Robots

ideas.title: Long-term autonomy with learned models
ideas.description: Integrate machine learning to learn sensor and motion models for long-term deployment.
ideas.sourceTitle: Probabilistic Robotics (Intelligent Robotics and Autonomous Agents)

ideas.title: Lifelong, Self-Tuning World Models via Bayesian Nonparametric Learning
ideas.description: This research direction combines learned models for long-term autonomy with Bayesian nonparametric mapping to create a unified framework for lifelong world modeling. The system continuously learns sensor and motion models while simultaneously employing nonparametric processes (e.g., Dirichlet Processes, Beta Processes) to flexibly grow, prune, and adapt its internal representation of landmarks, objects, and dynamic processes without predefined limits. This synergy allows the robot to not just adapt its parameters but to fundamentally restructure its understanding as the world changes, discovering new persistent entities and forgetting obsolete ones. The result is a robust, scalable autonomy system capable of deployment in complex, evolving environments for years, maintaining coherence and performance where fixed-model or parametric approaches would inevitably fail.

ideas.title: Open-world Landmark Semantics via Zero-Shot Visual Prototype Learning
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the 'unknown species discovery' problem in metagenomics to the challenge of open-world landmark semantics in computer vision and robotics. In metagenomics, DNA sequences from environmental samples are analyzed without a complete reference catalog, using marker genes and clustering to identify novel microbial taxa. Analogously, we propose a system that relaxes the closed-world assumption for landmarks by learning a foundational visual-semantic model from a broad but incomplete set of known landmarks and their textual descriptions. When encountering a new scene, the system embeds visual structures into this space, clusters them against known prototypes, and—for sufficiently novel clusters—hypothesizes new landmark types, generating descriptive semantic labels. This enables continuous discovery and semantic labeling of previously unknown landmark categories without pre-defined training data, moving from a fixed ontology to an open-world, extensible semantic map.

ideas.title: Dual: Voxel feature compression for registration
ideas.description: Use the registration framework to identify the most informative voxel features for data compression.
ideas.sourceTitle: Generalized-ICP

ideas.title: Bayesian Nonparametric Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Anatomical Landmark Discovery in Rare Skin Lesions
ideas.description: This research fuses meta-learning with Bayesian nonparametric models to create a system for few-shot adaptation to rare dermatological conditions. The meta-learning framework learns a prior over model parameters that facilitates rapid learning from few examples of a new skin cancer type. Crucially, the Bayesian nonparametric component models the visual representation of a lesion, allowing the model to infer a variable, unbounded number of semantically meaningful anatomical landmarks directly from the data during adaptation. This synergy creates a system that can both recognize new disease types and explain its decisions by discovering and adapting a flexible set of visual concepts, providing interpretability and robustness where traditional fixed-architecture models fail due to a lack of annotated landmarks for rare conditions.

ideas.title: Use active learning for efficient data labeling
ideas.description: Iteratively select the most informative images for expert labeling to optimize dataset growth.
ideas.sourceTitle: Dermatologist-level classification of skin cancer with deep neural networks

ideas.title: Benchmark suites are the only solution

ideas.title: Evidence-Based Open-World Landmark Acquisition via Persistent Utility Consensus
ideas.description: This system enables the discovery of new landmark types while rigorously guarding against semantic contamination. It treats potential new landmarks as hypotheses that must be validated against criteria of persistence, functional utility, and multi-agent consensus. A probabilistic world model maintains a distinction between stable landmark classes and unverified perceptual outliers. This allows for robust open-world semantic mapping where novel categories are integrated only when they demonstrably support reliable reasoning and task performance.

ideas.title: Active SLAM: choose actions to improve map

ideas.title: Distributed SLAM for Demographic-Invariant Sensing
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the need for large, diverse training datasets with passive SLAM for sensor networks. The core idea is to deploy a static, multi-modal sensor network (e.g., cameras, depth sensors, RF) in a complex environment to localize and track moving people. As the network operates, it applies SLAM principles not just to map the static geometry, but to build a vast, in-situ dataset of human appearances and motions under varying conditions. This continuously growing dataset is used to train embedded models in a self-supervised or federated learning framework, explicitly optimizing them to disentangle and become invariant to demographic attributes that typically bias sensing, thereby improving the network's generalizability and accuracy for all populations directly in the field.

ideas.title: Invert the expert dependency for training

ideas.sourceTitle: Dermatologist-level classification of skin cancer with deep neural networks

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Layer SLAM with Explicit Dynamic Entity Modeling and Forecasting
ideas.description: This research develops a SLAM framework that explicitly represents and reasons about different classes of environment dynamics in real-time. It constructs a layered world model consisting of a persistent static map, a semi-static layer for slow-changing elements, and a dynamic object layer with associated motion models. The system uses a combination of deep feature learning for semantic instance segmentation and a multi-hypothesis tracking filter to robustly associate measurements over time, preventing dynamic data from corrupting the static map. Furthermore, it learns and predicts short-term motion patterns of dynamic entities, enabling more accurate ego-motion estimation and proactive planning in complex, crowded scenes.

ideas.title: Active Open-World Landmark Discovery and Categorization
ideas.description: This research direction develops active perception policies for robotic agents operating under an open-world semantic assumption, where the set of possible landmark types is not fixed. The agent's control policy is designed to actively gather sensor data (e.g., by changing viewpoint or moving closer) specifically to resolve ambiguity between known landmark categories and to detect and form initial hypotheses about entirely unknown landmark types. This leads to a dual objective: minimizing state uncertainty for known semantics while maximizing information gain for discovering and characterizing novel semantic classes, enabling continuous, task-driven expansion of the agent's semantic map.

ideas.title: Meta-Selection of Heavy-Tailed Noise Models via Online Variational Inference
ideas.description: This system addresses the model selection problem inherent in using non-Gaussian noise by automating the choice and adaptation of the noise model itself. It places a Bayesian nonparametric prior over a flexible family of heavy-tailed distributions and their mixtures. Using online variational inference, the framework jointly estimates the system state and the posterior over noise model parameters and structure. This enables robust, real-time adaptation to observed sensor and motion anomalies while maintaining computational tractability and providing uncertainty quantification over the chosen noise model.

ideas.title: Collective, Real-Time Dynamic Scene Graph for Urban Multi-Agent Systems
ideas.description: This research direction develops a scalable, decentralized framework for multi-agent SLAM that generates a unified, city-scale dynamic scene graph. Each agent contributes real-time observations of both static infrastructure and non-stationary objects, with the system fusing these streams to maintain a globally consistent, live model of the entire metropolitan environment's state and its evolution. The key innovation is the synergistic loop: the real-time dynamic mapping from individual agents provides the dense, timely data needed to keep the collective model current, while the city-scale coordination from the swarm provides the persistent, multi-perspective tracking required to disambiguate and reliably predict complex dynamic interactions across vast areas. This enables applications like cooperative perception for autonomous vehicles, city-wide digital twin simulation, and real-time urban mobility optimization that are impossible with either standalone real-time mapping or large-scale static multi-agent SLAM.

ideas.title: Incorporate uncertainty quantification from Bayesian deep learning
ideas.description: Equip the model to estimate prediction uncertainty, flagging low-confidence cases for expert review.
ideas.sourceTitle: Dermatologist-level classification of skin cancer with deep neural networks

ideas.title: Density estimation for sample representation
ideas.description: Use kernel density estimation to represent the continuous belief from discrete samples.
ideas.sourceTitle: Monte Carlo Localization: Efficient Position Estimation for Mobile Robots

ideas.title: Hybrid Topological-Metric Navigation with Probabilistic Local Disambiguation
ideas.description: This approach creates a hierarchical world model where a coarse topological graph defines the connectivity of places, but each node is augmented with a lightweight, probabilistic metric model of its immediate surroundings. During navigation, the system uses the topological map for efficient long-horizon planning. When arriving at a hypothesized node, it employs the local metric model to perform probabilistic verification, distinguishing between perceptually similar locations and detecting if the local geometry has changed, thus updating the graph. This hybrid strategy maintains the scalability and abstraction of topological methods while directly addressing their fundamental vulnerability to aliasing and dynamics.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Diagnostic Fusion via Sensor-Augmented Neural Networks
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to improve diagnostic accuracy by fusing multi-modal data—specifically medical images and patient metadata (age, lesion history, etc.). Drawing an analogy from autonomous vehicle perception systems, which robustly fuse LiDAR, camera, radar, and GPS data using late-fusion neural network architectures, we adapt their solution. In autonomous vehicles, specialized sub-networks process each sensor type, and a fusion network dynamically integrates these features based on contextual confidence. Adapted to the medical context, the proposed solution involves designing a diagnostic model with parallel processing streams: a CNN for image data and an MLP for metadata. These streams feed into a learned fusion layer that dynamically weights the contribution of each modality based on the specific diagnostic context and data reliability, creating a more robust and accurate diagnostic system than simple early concatenation of raw data.

ideas.title: Noise-Aware Meta-Learning for Robust Training on Crowd-Sourced Signals
ideas.description: This approach reframes the problem of learning from weak labels as a meta-learning task over a population of noisy annotators. A primary model is not trained directly on the raw labels, but on a continuously refined training signal produced by a co-trained meta-learner. The meta-learner explicitly models the annotation process, estimating per-annotator reliability and systematic biases to deconvolve the underlying true label distribution. This allows the system to automatically identify and focus learning on high-confidence, consensus-driven examples from the crowd, building a robust model that inherits the scale of crowd-sourcing while mitigating its most corrupting inconsistencies.

ideas.title: Game theory for adversarial planning
ideas.description: Use game-theoretic models for navigation in environments with adversarial agents.
ideas.sourceTitle: Probabilistic Robotics (Intelligent Robotics and Autonomous Agents)

ideas.title: Robust State Estimation via Student's t-Distribution and Variational Inference
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of relaxing Gaussian noise assumptions by drawing an analogy from financial econometrics, where heavy-tailed distributions like Student's t are routinely used to model non-Gaussian asset returns. The analogous solution involves specifying a t-distribution for error terms and using robust Bayesian inference (e.g., EM, MCMC) for estimation. Adapted to robotics, the idea proposes to model sensor and motion noise with scaled Student's t-distributions within state estimation frameworks like Kalman filters or factor-graph-based SLAM. The heavier tails of the t-distribution make the model more robust to outliers. To handle the resulting non-Gaussian likelihood, the research would develop and apply efficient variational inference algorithms to jointly estimate the system state and the distribution parameters (e.g., degrees of freedom, scale matrix), creating a filter that is both adaptive and robust to real-world, heavy-tailed noise.

ideas.title: Localization in non-static, dynamic environments
ideas.description: Challenge the static world assumption by localizing amidst moving people and objects.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Data Association via Statistical Phylogenetics
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from evolutionary biology's homology problem to the challenge of unknown data association for landmarks. In phylogenetics, determining if similar traits in different species share a common ancestral origin is solved by probabilistic models that marginalize over possible sequence alignments and evolutionary histories. We propose to treat multiple observations of an unknown landmark set as analogous to species in a phylogenetic tree. The method will develop a joint probabilistic model to infer a latent 'ancestral' landmark configuration and the probabilistic correspondence (or 'alignment') of each observed landmark to this latent set. By marginalizing over all possible data associations, the approach can simultaneously estimate the most likely underlying structure and the uncertainty in landmark matching, providing a robust framework for when landmark identity is fundamentally unknown.

ideas.title: Swarm-Sourced Collective Localization via Weakly Supervised Learning
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training localization and mapping models for resource-constrained micro-robot swarms using the swarm's own collective sensor data as a form of weak, crowd-sourced supervision. Instead of relying on expert-curated labels or precise ground truth—which is infeasible at micro-scales and in unknown environments—the swarm uses consensus, redundancy, and relative motion within the collective to generate training targets. This allows the development of robust, adaptive algorithms that can infer position and map structure from severely limited sensor modalities (e.g., binary contact, coarse proximity) by learning from the swarm's own emergent spatio-temporal patterns. The result is a self-improving localization system where the robots' imperfect collective observations bootstrap a model more accurate than any individual robot could achieve or an expert could pre-design for such constrained platforms.

ideas.title: Task-Optimal Information Acquisition for Robust Decision-Making
ideas.description: This research designs control policies that actively gather sensory data to reduce uncertainty only where it matters for task success. The core innovation is a formal objective that maximizes the mutual information between selected actions and task-relevant latent state variables, conditioned on the agent's planned observations. This ensures the agent avoids pointless exploration and focuses its perceptual effort on disambiguating states that directly influence the optimal policy for achieving high reward. The resulting system balances the cost of information gathering against its expected contribution to robust, high-performance decision-making in partially observable environments.

ideas.title: Active Ambiguity-Aware Environment Identification via Multi-Hypothesis Inference
ideas.description: This system addresses the fundamental ill-posedness of inferring an environment from passive trajectories by not seeking a single answer. It uses a probabilistic, multi-hypothesis model to maintain a belief distribution over all plausible environmental configurations that are consistent with the observed robot data. Crucially, it integrates an active perception component, where the system plans and executes informative control sequences to gather new sensorimotor data that maximally discriminates between the top candidate environments. The final output is both a refined set of probable environment models and a quantitative measure of confidence and remaining ambiguity, enabling robust downstream decision-making.

ideas.title: Leverage Federated Learning and Synthetic Data Augmentation for Inclusive Dermatology AI
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from astronomy, where creating complete universal catalogs required federating data from multiple global telescopes and augmenting with simulations. The adapted research idea proposes to improve dermatology AI generalizability by implementing a two-pronged strategy: 1) A federated learning framework that trains models across decentralized, diverse clinical datasets worldwide without sharing raw patient data, thus accessing broader population and skin type representation. 2) The strategic use of advanced generative models to create synthetic dermatological images for under-represented conditions and skin tones, filling specific gaps in the real-world data. This combined approach of federated real data and targeted synthetic augmentation systematically expands dataset diversity and effective size, leading to more robust and equitable AI models.

ideas.title: Adapt Edge Computing from Autonomous Vehicles for Real-Time Mobile Diagnostics
ideas.description: This research adapts the edge computing solution from autonomous vehicle systems to optimize real-time mobile diagnostic tools. In autonomous vehicles, critical sensor data is processed locally at the 'edge' (in the car's computer) to enable instantaneous decisions like obstacle avoidance, while non-essential data is sent to the cloud. Similarly, we propose developing a mobile web application architecture where a core, lightweight diagnostic inference model runs directly on the mobile device. This enables immediate, latency-free preliminary analysis of patient data (e.g., ECG rhythm detection, rapid image triage) essential for clinical decision-making. The mobile edge client would handle the real-time imperative, synchronizing securely in the background with a cloud platform for heavier computational tasks, model retraining, and longitudinal data storage, thus ensuring robust performance even with unreliable network connectivity in hospitals or field settings.

ideas.title: Causally Regularized Multi-Modal Fusion with Adversarial Debiasing for Robust Generalization
ideas.description: This system integrates image data and patient metadata within a framework designed to prevent reliance on spurious correlations. It uses a causally-informed architecture that processes images and metadata through separate but interacting branches, regularized to ensure the image features are predictive of the disease independent of metadata. The training incorporates counterfactual augmentation of metadata and adversarial debiasing to strip away dataset-specific biases, and it is rigorously validated across diverse, held-out populations to guarantee that diagnostic accuracy generalizes beyond the training data distribution.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Swarm Localization with Anonymous Landmarks in Feature-Poor Environments
ideas.description: This research fuses micro-robot constraints with unknown data association to create a novel framework for swarm localization in environments devoid of unique, pre-defined features. The core challenge is that each micro-robot can only perceive anonymous, indistinct landmarks (e.g., generic intensity blobs, chemical concentration gradients) with severe noise and no inherent identity. The synergy emerges by developing ultra-lightweight probabilistic algorithms that run on individual robots to hypothesize landmark correspondences, while leveraging swarm consensus and relative inter-robot observations to jointly infer a globally consistent map and the poses of all agents. This enables emergent spatial awareness for the collective in scenarios where traditional SLAM or localization is impossible due to sensor poverty and perceptual ambiguity at the individual robot level.

ideas.title: Challenge mutual information as sole metric

ideas.title: Sparse Adversarial Perception and Planning (SAPP)
ideas.description: This research fuses compressed sensing and game theory to develop a framework for autonomous agents operating against adversaries in unknown or partially observable environments. The core idea is to formulate the sparse sensing problem—deciding what, when, and where to measure—as a strategic move in a game, where the objective is to maximize information gain about critical environmental features (like opponent positions or terrain hazards) while minimizing exposure and resource cost. This creates a closed-loop system where perception is no longer passive but an active, strategic component of adversarial planning. The result is a novel capability for resource-constrained agents to make provably efficient decisions under uncertainty, directly linking sparse representation theory to optimal adversarial behavior.

ideas.title: Hierarchical, Uncertainty-Aware Classification with Active Data Curation for Fine-Grained Dermatology
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simply adding more output classes by architecturally embedding awareness of data limitations and diagnostic uncertainty. It employs a hierarchical model that first reliably identifies broad lesion categories, then conditionally engages specialized sub-networks for fine-grained classification only when supported by sufficient evidence. The framework integrates an active learning component that flags low-confidence and potentially novel cases for expert consultation, dynamically curating a more balanced and informative training dataset. This results in a robust system that improves granularity where data permits while maintaining high reliability and explicitly quantifying its diagnostic confidence.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune-Inspired SLAM for Dynamic Environments
ideas.description: This research adapts principles from computational models of the adaptive immune system to solve the problem of real-time mapping in highly dynamic environments. The analogous solution from immunology involves maintaining a dynamic recognition model that distinguishes self from non-self through continuous learning and tolerance mechanisms. The adapted idea is to develop a bio-inspired SLAM algorithm where the core static map is protected by a process analogous to immunological tolerance. A parallel process, inspired by clonal selection and negative selection algorithms, continuously analyzes incoming sensor data. It classifies observations into persistent background ('self'), known dynamic objects (added to a 'tolerance library' to be ignored for static map updates), and novel dynamic objects ('non-self'). This allows the system to update a separate, real-time map of dynamic entities while preserving the integrity of the static environment map, enabling robust operation in crowded, non-stationary settings like public squares or busy factories.

ideas.title: A Probabilistic Ensemble for Differential Diagnosis Using Ecological Niche Modeling Principles
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that a single, definitive diagnosis must be the primary output of a clinical decision support system. Drawing an analogy from ecological niche modeling, where ensemble methods combine multiple algorithms to map probable species habitats with uncertainty, we propose a novel diagnostic framework. The system employs a diverse ensemble of diagnostic models (e.g., pathophysiological, pattern-recognition, probabilistic) that each process patient data. Their outputs are not collapsed into one answer but are synthesized into a visualized differential—a 'diagnostic niche'—that displays competing diagnostic hypotheses with associated confidence scores and model agreement metrics. This allows clinicians to review a landscape of possibilities weighted by algorithmic evidence, supporting a more nuanced, probabilistic, and transparent diagnostic reasoning process.

ideas.title: Active Online Sparsity Learning for Robust Compressive Environmental Mapping
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the fixed-basis limitation of classical compressed sensing by autonomously learning the optimal sparsifying transform for the specific environment during data acquisition. An online dictionary learning algorithm iteratively updates the signal model from compressive measurements, while an information-theoretic controller directs sensing resources to regions where the current model is most uncertain. This creates a closed-loop system that is robust to unknown or non-stationary scene structures, ensuring the efficiency of compressive sampling without pre-defined sparsity assumptions. The result is an adaptive mapping framework that maintains high fidelity in complex or evolving settings where naive compressed sensing would fail.

ideas.title: Dynamic sample size adaptation strategies

ideas.sourceTitle: Monte Carlo Localization: Efficient Position Estimation for Mobile Robots

ideas.title: Cognitive Map Navigation via Hippocampal Place Cell-Inspired Topological Graphs
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that precise metric maps are necessary for navigation by drawing an analogy from neuroscience. In the mammalian brain, the hippocampus supports navigation using place cells that encode topological relationships to landmarks rather than exact coordinates. The analogous solution is the construction of a cognitive map based on sequences of relational states. Adapted to robotics, this becomes a system that builds and navigates using a topological graph of recognizable sensory 'places' (nodes) and the proven traversability between them (edges). The agent's goal is to move from its current sensory signature to a target signature by following a path in this graph, bypassing the need for precise self-localization in a global metric frame and focusing purely on relational connectivity.

ideas.title: Adversarial Path Planning for Uncertainty-Aware Passive SLAM
ideas.description: This research direction combines Passive SLAM for sensor networks with deliberate uncertainty maximization to model an adversarial interaction. The core idea is to treat a network of static sensors performing SLAM on a moving target as a game, where the target's path is planned to actively maximize its own state uncertainty (e.g., position, velocity) within the sensor fusion framework. This dual perspective allows for the development of robust sensor network configurations and estimation algorithms that are explicitly tested against worst-case evasive maneuvers. The outcome is a co-design methodology for both resilient passive sensing systems and a theoretical understanding of optimal evasion under complex, multi-sensor observation models.

ideas.title: Continual Model Adaptation via Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological concept of succession to solve the problem of maintaining long-term autonomy with learned models. In ecology, ecosystems achieve long-term stability through a staged process of succession, where pioneer species prepare the ground for more complex communities. Analogously, a robotic system would implement a continual, staged learning architecture for its sensor and motion models. Initial 'pioneer' models, which are simple and robust to uncertainty, would be deployed in novel conditions. Their operation would generate labeled data and environmental characterization, which then actively facilitates the training and eventual transition to more accurate, specialized 'climax' models. This structured succession of models, coupled with mechanisms to detect environmental shifts triggering a new succession cycle, provides a framework for resilient, lifelong adaptation, preventing model obsolescence and enabling deployment over years in changing environments.

ideas.title: Bias-Aware Stratified Learning with Adversarial Dataset Auditing
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond passive dataset expansion to actively audit and curate data for equitable model generalization. An initial pipeline adversarially probes source datasets to quantify label quality and confounding variables across demographic and clinical subgroups. Training then uses a stratified optimization loss that minimizes worst-case subgroup error and leverages high-fidelity anchor sets to ensure reliable learning for all populations. The result is a model whose performance is robust and clinically credible across groups, not just on average, by directly addressing the root causes of bias amplification in large datasets.

ideas.title: Adaptive Particle Filters for Robust Physiological State Estimation Across Diverse Populations
ideas.description: This research fuses advanced particle filtering techniques from econometrics with the imperative for large, diverse biomedical datasets. The core idea is to develop adaptive particle filters that learn personalized proposal distributions and observation models from a vast, heterogeneous corpus of physiological time-series data (e.g., photoplethysmography, electrodermal activity). By training on data encompassing a wide range of populations, skin types, and environmental conditions, the filter's internal models become inherently robust to inter-subject variability and sensor noise. This enables high-fidelity, real-time estimation of latent physiological states for wearable and robotic health assistants, ensuring reliability and generalizability where traditional model-based filters or models trained on narrow datasets fail.

ideas.title: City-scale Multi-agent SLAM via Distributed Consensus and Gossip Protocols
ideas.description: This research expands the original city-scale multi-agent SLAM idea by drawing an analogy from distributed systems and peer-to-peer networking. The analogous problem of maintaining a consistent global state across thousands of unreliable, decentralized nodes (as in P2P file-sharing networks) is solved using gossip protocols for efficient data dissemination and consensus algorithms for state agreement. Adapting this solution, we propose a SLAM framework where each vehicle acts as an autonomous agent that performs local SLAM and periodically exchanges compact spatial state summaries (map digests) with nearby vehicles via vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication, mimicking a gossip protocol. A lightweight, distributed consensus mechanism, potentially hosted on edge cloudlets or a robust subset of vehicles, is employed to resolve conflicts and merge high-confidence updates into a globally consistent, scalable, and decentralized city map. This approach directly addresses the SCALING_DUAL dimension by distributing both the computational load and the map maintenance across the entire fleet, enabling robust, large-scale collaborative mapping.

ideas.title: Probabilistic Lesion Trajectory Inference from Sparse, Irregular Temporal Imaging
ideas.description: This system is designed to analyze medical lesion progression using real-world, sparse temporal image data. It first aligns and normalizes sequential images into a learned canonical feature space to overcome technical variability. A probabilistic temporal model then interpolates a continuous trajectory of disease state from these irregular observations, estimating both the most likely progression path and the confidence intervals around that estimate. This approach provides clinically actionable predictions of future change and quantifies diagnostic uncertainty, directly addressing the challenges of inconsistent clinical imaging schedules.

ideas.title: Inverse Validation: Inferring Clinical Environment Models from Prospective AI Diagnostic Trails
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework where a medical AI model (e.g., for dermatology) is deployed in a prospective, multi-center clinical trial. Instead of just reporting aggregate performance metrics versus clinicians, the trial data—including model predictions, outcomes, and limited site metadata—is treated as trajectory data from an 'AI agent' navigating a hidden clinical environment. An inverse problem approach is then applied to infer a probabilistic model of the latent environmental factors (e.g., prevalence of rare conditions, imaging protocol variations, referral biases, operator skill) that best explain the observed spatial and temporal patterns in the AI's performance. This inferred environmental model provides a causal, interpretable layer to validation, identifying not just where the AI fails, but why, and under what specific environmental conditions it can be trusted.

ideas.title: Inverse Terrain Reconstruction via Trajectory Spectral Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from seismic tomography in geophysics to the robotics inverse problem of environment identification. In the analogous field, the internal structure of the Earth is inferred from the propagation of seismic waves. Here, the robot's trajectory (its path and dynamic state over time) is treated as a 'wave' propagating through an environmental 'medium' with unknown spatially varying properties like terrain type, friction, and obstacle density. The adapted solution involves analyzing the spectral signatures, timing, and energy expenditure of the trajectory to perform an inverse reconstruction. This allows us to infer a probabilistic environmental model—essentially performing 'terrain tomography'—from the observed robot motion data alone, scaling the inference to complex, large-scale environments.

ideas.title: Strategic Entropy Shaping for Adversarial Probing and Privacy
ideas.description: This approach designs action policies that deliberately maximize an agent's state uncertainty, but only as a sub-goal within a constrained, purposeful framework. For privacy, it generates trajectories that confuse external observers while reaching a destination. For security testing, it crafts inputs that maximally confuse a sensor system's classification, revealing its blind spots. The core advance is the formal treatment of uncertainty not as an end in itself, but as a strategic resource to be shaped for specific adversarial or information-gathering objectives under constraints.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Dermatology AI with Adaptive Abstention for Consumer-Grade Images
ideas.description: This research develops a skin lesion analysis system that is not only robust to variable image quality but also self-aware, capable of rejecting predictions it deems unreliable. The model jointly learns to classify lesions and to estimate a confidence or feature-adequacy score based on the input's informational content. When the estimated confidence falls below a calibrated safety threshold, the system abstains from diagnosis and provides actionable feedback, such as requesting a new image with specific improvements. This approach prioritizes safe deployment by preventing overconfident errors on poor-quality inputs that no model could accurately interpret.

ideas.title: Sparse Environmental Mapping via Genomic Sequencing Principles
ideas.description: This idea applies the principles of sparse genomic sequencing to the problem of efficient environmental mapping. In genomics, complete DNA sequences are reconstructed from randomly sampled fragments using computational assembly algorithms that leverage structural priors. Adapted to mapping, a mobile platform would collect sparse, random spatial measurements (disjoint point clouds, intermittent images) instead of exhaustive scans. A compressed sensing-inspired reconstruction algorithm, incorporating priors about common environmental geometries and continuities, would then assemble a complete, accurate 3D map from this minimal dataset. This approach aims to achieve efficient environmental representation by drastically reducing the volume of raw data required for reconstruction, analogous to how modern sequencing reconstructs genomes without full, sequential reads.

ideas.title: Real-time, quality-adaptive mobile diagnostics with active capture guidance
ideas.description: This research develops a real-time mobile diagnostic web application that integrates a novel, dual-function AI model. The model simultaneously performs two tasks: it assesses the diagnostic quality of a live video stream from a consumer-grade smartphone camera in real-time, and it provides immediate, actionable visual or auditory feedback to the clinician or patient to adjust distance, angle, lighting, or focus. By fusing real-time optimization with robustness to poor quality, the system actively mitigates the root cause of low-quality inputs during the capture process itself. This creates a closed-loop, guided acquisition system that elevates the reliability of point-of-care diagnostics beyond what passive analysis or post-hoc robustness alone can achieve.
